title: Contrastive Multi-Modal Hypergraph Reasoning for 3D Crowd Mesh Recovery
summary: Multi-person 3D reconstruction is pivotal for real-world interaction analysis, yet remains challenging due to severe occlusions and depth ambiguity. Current approaches typically rely on single-modality inputs, which inherently lack geometric guidance. Furthermore, these methods often reconstruct subjects in isolation, neglecting the collective group context essential for resolving ambiguities in crowded scenes. To address these limitations, we propose Contrastive Multi-modal Hypergraph Reasoning to synergize semantic, geometric, and pose cues for crowd reconstruction. We first initialize robust node representations by combining RGB features, geometric priors, and occlusion-aware incomplete poses. Additionally, we introduce a pelvis depth indicator as a global spatial anchor, aligning visual features with a metric-scale-agnostic depth ordering. Subsequently, we construct a shared-topology hypergraph that moves beyond pairwise constraints to model higher-order crowd dynamics. To improve feature fusion, we design a hypergraph-based contrastive learning scheme that jointly enhances intra-modal discriminability and enforces cross-modal orthogonality. This mechanism enables the network to propagate global context effectively, allowing it to infer missing information even under severe occlusion. Extensive experiments on the Panoptic and GigaCrowd benchmarks confirm that our method achieves new state-of-the-art performance. Code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/SunMH-try/CoMHR.

AI.tldr: Proposes CoMHR, a contrastive multi-modal hypergraph reasoning framework that fuses semantic, geometric, and pose cues with a pelvis depth anchor and hypergraph-based contrastive learning to achieve state-of-the-art multi-person 3D reconstruction under occlusion.
AI.motivation: Current multi-person 3D reconstruction methods use single-modality inputs lacking geometric guidance and reconstruct subjects in isolation, ignoring group context needed to resolve occlusions and depth ambiguity in crowded scenes.
AI.method: 1) Initialize robust node representations from RGB, geometric priors, and occlusion-aware poses. 2) Introduce pelvis depth indicator as global spatial anchor. 3) Build shared-topology hypergraph for higher-order crowd dynamics. 4) Design hypergraph-based contrastive learning for intra-modal discriminability and cross-modal orthogonality.
AI.result: Achieves new state-of-the-art performance on Panoptic and GigaCrowd benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Multi-modal hypergraph reasoning with contrastive learning effectively propagates global context and infers missing information under severe occlusion, advancing crowd-level 3D reconstruction.

title: Few Channels Draw The Whole Picture: Revealing Massive Activations in Diffusion Transformers
summary: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) and related flow-based architectures are now among the strongest text-to-image generators, yet the internal mechanisms through which prompts shape image semantics remain poorly understood. In this work, we study massive activations: a small subset of hidden-state channels whose responses are consistently much larger than the rest. We show that, despite their sparsity, these few channels effectively draw the whole picture, in three complementary senses. First, they are functionally critical: a controlled disruption probe that zeroes the massive channels causes a sharp collapse in generation quality, while disrupting an equally-sized set of low-statistic channels has marginal effect. Second, they are spatially organized: restricting image-stream tokens to massive channels and clustering them yields coherent partitions that closely align with the main subject and salient regions, exposing a structured spatial code hidden inside an apparently outlier-like subspace. Third, they are transferable: transporting massive activations from one prompt-conditioned trajectory into another, shifts the final image toward the source prompt while preserving substantial content from the target, producing localized semantic interpolation rather than unstructured pixel blending. We exploit this property in two use cases: text-conditioned and image-conditioned semantic transport, where massive activations transport enables prompt interpolation and subject-driven generation without any additional training. Together, these results recast massive activations not as activation anomalies, but as a sparse prompt-conditioned carrier subspace that organizes and controls semantic information in modern DiT models.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that massive activations—a small subset of hidden-state channels with unusually high responses—serve as a sparse, structured carrier subspace that organizes and controls semantic information in Diffusion Transformers (DiTs), enabling functional control, spatial organization, and transferable semantic transport for text-to-image generation.
AI.motivation: While Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) and flow-based architectures are state-of-the-art for text-to-image generation, how prompts shape image semantics internally remains poorly understood. The authors aim to demystify this black box by studying massive activations.
AI.method: The authors analyze massive activations through three lenses: (1) functional criticality via controlled disruption probing, (2) spatial organization by clustering image-stream tokens restricted to massive channels, and (3) transferability by transporting massive activations between prompt-conditioned trajectories.
AI.result: Massive activations are (i) functionally critical—disrupting them collapses generation quality while disrupting low-statistic channels has marginal effect; (ii) spatially organized—clustering them yields coherent partitions aligned with main subjects; (iii) transferable—transporting them between trajectories enables localized semantic interpolation.
AI.conclusion: Massive activations are not merely activation anomalies but a sparse, prompt-conditioned carrier subspace that organizes and controls semantic information in DiTs. This enables practical applications like text-conditioned and image-conditioned semantic transport without additional training.

title: CineMesh4D: Personalized 4D Whole Heart Reconstruction from Sparse Cine MRI
summary: Accurate 3D+t whole-heart mesh reconstruction from cine MRI is a clinically crucial yet technically challenging task. The difficulty of this task arises from two coupled factors: inherently sparse sampling of 3D cardiac anatomy by 2D image slices and the tight coupling between cardiac shape and motion. Current cardiac image-to-mesh approaches typically reconstruct only a subset of cardiac chambers or a single phase of the cardiac cycle. In this work, we propose CineMesh4D, a novel end-to-end 4D (3D+t) pipeline that directly reconstructs patient-specific whole-heart mesh from multi-view 2D cine MRI via cross-domain mapping. Specifically, we introduce a differentiable rendering loss that enables supervision of 3D+t whole-heart mesh from multi-view sparse contours of cine MRI. Furthermore, we develop a dual-context temporal block that fuses global and local cardiac temporal information to capture high-dimensional sequential patterns. In quantitative and qualitative evaluations, CineMesh4D outperforms existing approaches in terms of reconstruction quality and motion consistency, providing a practical pathway for personalized real-time cardiac assessment. The code will be publicly released once the manuscript is accepted.

AI.tldr: CineMesh4D is an end-to-end 4D pipeline that directly reconstructs patient-specific whole-heart mesh from multi-view 2D cine MRI via cross-domain mapping, using differentiable rendering loss and dual-context temporal blocks, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Current methods for 3D+t whole-heart mesh reconstruction from cine MRI face challenges due to sparse 2D slice sampling and tight coupling between shape and motion, and existing approaches typically reconstruct only a subset of chambers or a single cardiac phase, lacking full 4D reconstruction.
AI.method: CineMesh4D is an end-to-end 4D pipeline that: (1) uses a differentiable rendering loss for supervising 3D+t mesh from multi-view sparse contours of cine MRI, and (2) develops a dual-context temporal block fusing global and local cardiac temporal information to capture high-dimensional sequential patterns.
AI.result: CineMesh4D outperforms existing approaches in reconstruction quality and motion consistency on quantitative and qualitative evaluations.
AI.conclusion: CineMesh4D provides a practical pathway for personalized real-time cardiac assessment by enabling accurate whole-heart 4D reconstruction from cine MRI.

title: Unified Pix Token And Word Token Generative Language Model
summary: Since the emergence of Vision Transformer (ViT), it has been widely used in generative language model and generative visual model. Especially in the current state-of-art open source multimodal models, ViT obtained by CLIP or SigLIP method serves as the vision encoder backbone to help them acquire visual understanding capabilities. But this method leads to limitations in visual understanding for details, such as difficulty in recognizing small text or numbers in images. To address these issues, we propose a new model to unify pix token and word token into the generative language model. The new model also features with each pix of image having its own token embedding, color folding, global conditional attention approximation and image unsupervised pretraining. We conducted image unsupervised pretraining experiments using our new model to explore its potential. The experimental results show that it has good performance even in small model and with limited training data. We believe our model also conforms to the scaling law, as long as model parameters and training data increased, its performance will continue to improve.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new vision-language model that replaces traditional ViT-based encoders with per-pixel token embeddings, color folding, and global conditional attention, enabling better fine-grained visual understanding (e.g., small text/numbers) and showing strong performance even with small models and limited data, with promise of scaling.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal models rely on CLIP/SigLIP-based ViT vision encoders, which struggle with fine-grained visual details like small text or numbers in images.
AI.method: Proposes a new model that unifies pixel tokens and word tokens into a generative language model, featuring per-pixel token embeddings, color folding, global conditional attention approximation, and image unsupervised pretraining.
AI.result: Image unsupervised pretraining experiments show good performance even with small model size and limited training data; the model appears to follow scaling laws.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach overcomes ViT-based limitations in detail recognition and shows potential for continued improvement with more parameters and data.

title: PVRF: All-in-one Adverse Weather Removal via Prior-modulated and Velocity-constrained Rectified Flow
summary: Adverse weather removal (AWR) in real-world images remains challenging due to heterogeneous and unseen degradations, while distortion-driven training often yields overly smooth results. We propose PVRF, a unified framework that integrates zero-shot soft weather perceptions with velocity-constrained rectified-flow refinement. PVRF introduces an AWR-specific question answering module (AWR-QA) that uses frozen vision--language models (VLMs) to estimate soft probabilities of weather types and low-level attribute scores. These perceptions condition restoration networks via attribute-modulated normalization (AMN) and weather-weighted adapters (WWA), producing an anchor estimate for refinement. We then learn a terminal-consistent residual rectified flow with perception-adaptive source perturbation and a terminal-consistent velocity parameterization to stabilize learning near the terminal regime. Extensive experiments show that PVRF improves both fidelity and perceptual quality over state-of-the-art baselines, with strong cross-dataset generalization on single and combined degradations. Code will be released at https://github.com/dongw22/PVRF.

AI.tldr: PVRF is a unified framework for adverse weather removal that integrates zero-shot soft weather perceptions from frozen vision-language models with velocity-constrained rectified-flow refinement, significantly improving fidelity and perceptual quality over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing AWR methods struggle with heterogeneous/unseen weather degradations and produce overly smooth results due to distortion-driven training. The authors aim to leverage semantic understanding from VLMs to guide restoration.
AI.method: (1) AWR-QA module using frozen VLMs to estimate soft weather probabilities and attribute scores; (2) Attribute-modulated normalization (AMN) and weather-weighted adapters (WWA) to condition restoration networks; (3) Terminal-consistent residual rectified flow with perception-adaptive source perturbation and terminal-consistent velocity parameterization.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both fidelity and perceptual quality, with strong cross-dataset generalization on single and combined degradations.
AI.conclusion: Integrating soft semantic perceptions from VLMs with rectified-flow refinement is an effective paradigm for unified adverse weather removal.

title: Evolving Layer-Specific Scalar Functions for Hardware-Aware Transformer Adaptation
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) achieve state-of-the-art performance on challenging vision tasks, but their deployment on edge devices is severely hindered by the computational complexity and global reduction bottleneck imposed by layer normalization. Recent methods attempt to bypass this by replacing normalization layers with hardware-friendly scalar approximations. However, these homogeneous replacements do not optimally fit to all layers' behaviour and rely on expensive model retraining. In this work, we propose a highly efficient, hardware-aware framework that utilizes genetic programming (GP) to evolve heterogeneous, layer-specific scalar functions directly from pre-trained weights. Coupled with a novel post-training re-alignment strategy, our approach eliminates the need to retrain models from scratch entirely. Our evolved expressions accurately approximate the target normalization behaviours, capturing $91.6\%$ of the variance ($R^2$) compared to only $70.2\%$ for homogeneous baselines, allowing our modified architecture to recover $84.25\%$ Top-1 ImageNet-1K accuracy in only 20 epochs. By preserving this performance while eliminating the global reduction bottleneck, our approach establishes a highly favourable trade-off between arithmetic complexity and off-chip memory traffic, removing a primary barrier to the efficient deployment of ViTs on edge accelerators.

AI.tldr: A genetic programming framework that evolves heterogeneous, layer-specific scalar functions to replace normalization layers in Vision Transformers, eliminating computational bottlenecks and achieving strong accuracy recovery without full model retraining.
AI.motivation: Vision Transformers achieve state-of-the-art performance but are hindered by computational complexity and global reduction bottlenecks from layer normalization when deployed on edge devices.
AI.method: Uses genetic programming to evolve heterogeneous, layer-specific scalar functions from pre-trained weights, coupled with a post-training re-alignment strategy to avoid full retraining.
AI.result: Achieves 91.6% variance capture (R²) vs 70.2% for homogeneous baselines, recovers 84.25% Top-1 ImageNet-1K accuracy in only 20 epochs.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a favourable trade-off between arithmetic complexity and off-chip memory traffic, removing a key barrier to efficient ViT deployment on edge accelerators.

title: CurveBench: A Benchmark for Exact Topological Reasoning over Nested Jordan Curves
summary: We introduce CurveBench, a benchmark for hierarchical topological reasoning from visual input. CurveBench consists of \textbf{756 images} of pairwise non-intersecting Jordan curves across easy, polygonal, topographic-inspired, maze-like, and dense counting configurations. Each image is annotated with a rooted tree encoding the containment relations between planar regions. We formulate the task as structured prediction: given an image, a model must recover the full rooted containment tree induced by the curves. Despite the visual simplicity of the task, the strongest evaluated model, Gemini 3.1 Pro, achieves only \textbf{71.1\%} tree-generation accuracy on CurveBench-Easy and \textbf{19.1\%} on CurveBench-Hard. We further demonstrate benchmark utility through RLVR-style fine-tuning of open-weight vision-language models. Our trained Qwen3-VL-8B model improves over \texttt{Qwen-3-VL-8B-Thinking} from \textbf{2.8\%} to \textbf{33.3\%} tree-generation accuracy on CurveBench-Easy, exceeding GPT-5.4 and Claude Opus 4.5 under our evaluation protocol. The remaining gap, especially on CurveBench-Hard, shows that exact topology-aware visual reasoning remains far from solved.

AI.tldr: CurveBench is a benchmark of 756 images of nested Jordan curves testing hierarchical topological reasoning. The best model (Gemini 3.1 Pro) achieves only 71.1% accuracy on easy and 19.1% on hard subsets. Fine-tuning Qwen3-VL-8B improves from 2.8% to 33.3% on easy, but large gaps remain on hard tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-language models struggle with exact topological reasoning. The authors introduce CurveBench to systematically evaluate and improve models' ability to recover containment hierarchies from visual inputs.
AI.method: CurveBench contains 756 images of non-intersecting Jordan curves across five difficulty levels (easy, polygonal, topographic, maze-like, dense counting). Each image has a ground-truth rooted containment tree. The task is structured prediction: given an image, output the full containment tree. Evaluation uses tree-generation accuracy, and RLVR-style fine-tuning is applied to open-weight VLMs.
AI.result: Gemini 3.1 Pro achieves 71.1% (easy) and 19.1% (hard). RLVR fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B rises from 2.8% to 33.3% on easy, surpassing GPT-5.4 and Claude Opus 4.5. Hard subset remains challenging for all models.
AI.conclusion: Exact topology-aware visual reasoning remains unsolved. While fine-tuning yields large gains on easier tasks, the hard subset gap indicates fundamental limitations in current VLM architectures for hierarchical topological reasoning.

title: Venus-DeFakerOne: Unified Fake Image Detection & Localization
summary: In recent years, the rapid evolution of generative AI has fundamentally reshaped the paradigm of image forgery, breaking the traditional boundaries between document editing, natural image manipulation, DeepFake generation, and full-image AIGC synthesis. Despite this shift toward unified forgery generation, existing research in Fake Image Detection and Localization (FIDL) remains fragmented. This creates a mismatch between increasingly unified forgery generation mechanisms and the domain-specific detection paradigm. Bridging this mismatch poses two key challenges for FIDL: understanding cross-domain artifacts transfer and interference, and building a high-capacity unified foundation model for joint detection and localization. To address these challenges, we propose DeFakerOne, a data-centric, unified FIDL foundation model integrating InternVL2 and SAM2. DeFakerOne enables simultaneous image-level detection and pixel-level forgery localization across diverse scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DeFakerOne achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming baselines on 39 forgery detection benchmarks and 9 localization benchmarks. Furthermore, the model exhibits superior robustness against real-world perturbations and state-of-the-art generators such as GPT-Image-2. Finally, we provide a systematic analysis of data scaling laws, cross-domain artifacts transfer-interference patterns, the necessity of fine-grained supervision, and the original resolution artifacts preservation, highlighting the design principles for scalable, robust, and unified FIDL.

AI.tldr: DeFakerOne is a unified foundation model integrating InternVL2 and SAM2 for joint fake image detection and pixel-level forgery localization, achieving SOTA across 39 detection and 9 localization benchmarks with strong robustness against modern generators like GPT-Image-2.
AI.motivation: Generative AI has unified forgery generation across document editing, natural image manipulation, DeepFake, and AIGC synthesis, but existing Fake Image Detection and Localization (FIDL) research remains fragmented. This mismatch creates challenges in cross-domain artifact transfer/interference and the need for a unified high-capacity foundation model.
AI.method: Proposes DeFakerOne, a data-centric unified FIDL foundation model that integrates InternVL2 (vision-language backbone) and SAM2 (segmentation model) to simultaneously perform image-level detection and pixel-level forgery localization across diverse forgery domains.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on 39 forgery detection benchmarks and 9 localization benchmarks; exhibits superior robustness against real-world perturbations and advanced generators like GPT-Image-2.
AI.conclusion: Provides systematic analysis of data scaling laws, cross-domain artifact transfer-interference patterns, the necessity of fine-grained supervision, and original resolution artifacts preservation, offering design principles for scalable, robust, and unified FIDL.

title: DUET: Dual-Paradigm Adaptive Expert Triage with Single-cell Inductive Prior for Spatial Transcriptomics Prediction
summary: Inferring spatially resolved gene expression from histology images offers a cost-effective complement to spatial transcriptomics (ST). However, existing methods reduce this task to a simple morphology-to-expression mapping, where visual similarity does not guarantee molecular consistency. Meanwhile, single-cell data has amassed rich resources far surpassing the scale of ST data, yet it remains underexplored in vision-omics modeling. Furthermore, current approaches commit to a monolithic paradigm with bottlenecks, unable to balance expressive flexibility with biological fidelity. To bridge these gaps, we propose DUET, a novel dual-paradigm framework that synergizes parametric prediction and memory-based retrieval under cellular inductive priors. DUET implements a parallel regression-retrieval paradigm, adaptively reconciling the outputs of its complementary pathways. To mitigate aleatoric vision ambiguity, we incorporate large-scale single-cell references to impose molecular states as biological constraints for faithful learning. Building upon structural refinement, we further design a lightweight adapter to dynamically assign branch preference across spatial contexts to achieve optimal performance. Extensive experiments on three public datasets across varied gene scales demonstrate that DUET achieves SOTA performance, with consistent gains contributed by each proposed component. Code is available at https://github.com/Junchao-Zhu/DUET

AI.tldr: DUET is a dual-paradigm framework combining parametric prediction and memory-based retrieval with single-cell references to improve spatially resolved gene expression inference from histology images, achieving SOTA performance.
AI.motivation: Existing methods reduce gene expression inference to morphology-to-expression mapping where visual similarity doesn't guarantee molecular consistency, and they fail to leverage rich single-cell data resources or balance expressive flexibility with biological fidelity.
AI.method: DUET uses a parallel regression-retrieval paradigm synergizing parametric prediction and memory-based retrieval under cellular inductive priors, incorporates large-scale single-cell references as biological constraints, and includes a lightweight adapter for dynamic branch preference across spatial contexts.
AI.result: Experiments on three public datasets across varied gene scales show DUET achieves state-of-the-art performance with consistent gains from each proposed component.
AI.conclusion: DUET effectively bridges gaps in spatial gene expression inference by integrating complementary paradigms and single-cell knowledge, offering a more biologically faithful and flexible approach.

title: Bridging the Rural Healthcare Gap: A Cascaded Edge-Cloud Architecture for Automated Retinal Screening
summary: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) is one of the leading causes of preventable blindness, yet rural regions often lack the specialists and infrastructure needed for early detection. Although cloud-based deep learning systems offer high accuracy, they face significant challenges in these settings due to high latency, limited bandwidth, and high data transmission costs. To address these challenges, we propose a two-tier edge-cloud cascade on the public APTOS 2019 Blindness Detection dataset. Tier 1 runs a lightweight MobileNetV3-small model on a local clinic device to perform a binary triage between Referable DR (Classes 2-4) and Non-referable DR (Classes 0-1). Tier 2 runs a RETFoundDINOv2 model in the cloud for ordinal severity grading, but only on the subset of images flagged as referable by Tier 1. On a stratified APTOS test split of 733 images, Tier 1 reaches 98.99% sensitivity and 84.37% specificity at a validation-tuned high-sensitivity threshold. The default cascade forwards 49.52% of test images to Tier 2, reducing cloud calls by 50.48% relative to using a cloud-based model for all images. In the deployed 4-class output space (Class 0-1 / Class 2 / Class 3 / Class 4), the cascade obtains 80.49% accuracy and 0.8167 quadratic weighted kappa; the cloud-only baseline obtains 80.76% accuracy and 0.8184 quadratic weighted kappa. On APTOS, the cascade cuts cloud use by about half with a modest drop in grading performance. Index Terms: Diabetic Retinopathy, Edge-Cloud Cascade, MobileNetV3-small, RETFound-DINOv2, Retinal Screening, tele-ophthalmology

AI.tldr: A two-tier edge-cloud cascade system for DR detection using lightweight MobileNetV3-small at the edge and RETFoundDINOv2 in the cloud, reducing cloud calls by ~50% with minimal performance loss.
AI.motivation: Cloud-based deep learning systems face high latency, limited bandwidth, and high data transmission costs in rural regions lacking DR specialists. The authors aim to address these deployment challenges.
AI.method: Tier 1: MobileNetV3-small on local device for binary triage (Referable vs Non-referable DR). Tier 2: RETFoundDINOv2 cloud model for ordinal severity grading on images flagged as referable. Evaluated on APTOS 2019 dataset.
AI.result: Tier 1: 98.99% sensitivity, 84.37% specificity. Cascade cuts cloud use by 50.48% with 80.49% accuracy (vs 80.76% cloud-only) and 0.8167 kappa (vs 0.8184 cloud-only).
AI.conclusion: The edge-cloud cascade reduces cloud calls by about half with only a modest drop in grading performance, making it suitable for resource-constrained rural settings.

title: SToRe3D: Sparse Token Relevance in ViTs for Efficient Multi-View 3D Object Detection
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) enable strong multi-view 3D detection but are limited by high inference latency from dense token and query processing across multiple views and large 3D regions. Existing sparsity methods, designed mainly for 2D vision, prune or merge image tokens but do not extend to full-model sparsity or address 3D object queries. We introduce SToRe3D, a relevance-aligned sparsity framework that jointly selects 2D image tokens and 3D object queries while storing filtered features for reactivation. Mutual 2D-3D relevance heads allocate compute to driving-critical content and preserve other embeddings. Evaluated on nuScenes and our new nuScenes-Relevance benchmark, SToRe3D achieves up to 3x faster inference with marginal accuracy loss, establishing real-time large-scale ViT-based 3D detection while maintaining accuracy on planning-critical agents.

AI.tldr: SToRe3D jointly prunes 2D image tokens and 3D object queries to accelerate ViT-based multi-view 3D detection up to 3x faster with minimal accuracy loss, using mutual relevance heads and stored features for reactivation.
AI.motivation: Vision Transformers for multi-view 3D detection suffer from high inference latency due to dense token/query processing across multiple views and large 3D regions; existing sparsity methods are limited to 2D vision and don't address full-model sparsity or 3D queries.
AI.method: Proposes SToRe3D, a relevance-aligned sparsity framework that jointly selects 2D image tokens and 3D object queries, using mutual 2D-3D relevance heads to allocate compute to critical content while storing filtered features for reactivation.
AI.result: Up to 3x faster inference with marginal accuracy loss on nuScenes and nuScenes-Relevance benchmarks, enabling real-time large-scale ViT-based 3D detection.
AI.conclusion: SToRe3D enables real-time large-scale ViT-based 3D detection while maintaining accuracy on planning-critical agents.

title: ProtoMedAgent: Multimodal Clinical Interpretability via Privacy-Aware Agentic Workflows
summary: While interpretable prototype networks offer compelling case-based reasoning for clinical diagnostics, their raw continuous outputs lack the semantic structure required for medical documentation. Bridging this gap via standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) routinely triggers ``retrieval sycophancy,'' where Large Language Models (LLMs) hallucinate post-hoc rationalizations to align with visual predictions. We introduce ProtoMedAgent, a framework that formalizes multimodal clinical reporting as an iterative, zero-gradient test-time optimization problem over a strict neuro-symbolic bottleneck. Operating on a frozen prototype backbone, we distill latent visual and tabular features into a discrete semantic memory. Online generation is strictly constrained by exact set-theoretic differentials and a reflective Scribe-Critic loop, mathematically precluding unsupported narrative claims. To safely bound data disclosure, we introduce a semantic privacy gate governed by $k$-anonymity and $\ell$-diversity. Evaluated on a 4,160-patient clinical cohort, ProtoMedAgent achieves 91.2\% Comparison Set Faithfulness where it fundamentally outperforms standard RAG (46.2\%). ProtoMedAgent additionally leverages a binding $\ell$-diversity phase transition to systematically reduce artifact-level membership inference risks by an absolute 9.8\%.

AI.tldr: ProtoMedAgent is a framework that converts prototype network outputs into structured clinical reports using a neuro-symbolic bottleneck and Scribe-Critic loop, achieving 91.2% faithfulness vs. 46.2% for standard RAG, while reducing membership inference risks by 9.8% via k-anonymity and ℓ-diversity.
AI.motivation: Interpretable prototype networks produce clinically useful case-based reasoning but output raw continuous features lacking semantic structure for medical documentation. Standard RAG bridging causes "retrieval sycophancy"—LLMs hallucinate post-hoc rationalizations to match visual predictions.
AI.method: ProtoMedAgent formalizes multimodal clinical reporting as an iterative, zero-gradient test-time optimization over a neuro-symbolic bottleneck. It distills latent features into discrete semantic memory, constrains generation via exact set-theoretic differentials and a reflective Scribe-Critic loop, and applies a semantic privacy gate with k-anonymity and ℓ-diversity.
AI.result: On a 4,160-patient cohort, ProtoMedAgent achieves 91.2% Comparison Set Faithfulness vs. 46.2% for standard RAG, and reduces artifact-level membership inference risks by an absolute 9.8% via an ℓ-diversity phase transition.
AI.conclusion: ProtoMedAgent demonstrates that strict neuro-symbolic constraints can overcome retrieval sycophancy in clinical NLP, delivering both faithful generation and provable privacy guarantees simultaneously.

title: PanoPlane: Plane-Aware Panoramic Completion for Sparse-View Indoor 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: We present PanoPlane, an approach for high-fidelity sparse-view indoor novel view synthesis that reconstructs closed room geometry via panoramic scene completion. Unlike perspective-based methods that generate training views from limited fields of view, PanoPlane leverages $360^{\circ}$ panoramic completion to condition the generative process on the full spatial layout. We propose Layout Anchored Attention Steering, a training-free mechanism that steers attention within the diffusion model's internal representation toward scene's detected planar surfaces at inference time. By directing each unobserved region's attention toward geometrically consistent observed content, our method replaces unconstrained hallucination with grounded surface extrapolation. The resulting panoramic completions provide supervision for 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling accurate novel-view synthesis across unobserved regions from as few as three input views. Experiments on Replica, ScanNet++, and Matterport3D demonstrate state-of-the-art novel view synthesis quality across 3, 6, and 9 input views, achieving up to $+17.8\%$ improvement in PSNR over the current state-of-the-art baseline without any training or fine-tuning of the diffusion model.

AI.tldr: PanoPlane enables high-fidelity indoor novel view synthesis from sparse views by using panoramic scene completion and a training-free attention steering mechanism that grounds unobserved regions to detected planar surfaces, achieving up to +17.8% PSNR improvement without any diffusion model fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing perspective-based methods for novel view synthesis are limited by narrow fields of view, leading to unconstrained hallucination in unobserved regions. The authors propose leveraging 360° panoramic completion to better capture full spatial layout from sparse inputs.
AI.method: PanoPlane uses (1) 360° panoramic completion to condition the generative process on full spatial layout, (2) Layout Anchored Attention Steering (LAAS) — a training-free mechanism that steers diffusion model attention toward detected planar surfaces at inference time, and (3) uses the completed panoramas as supervision for 3D Gaussian Splatting.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on Replica, ScanNet++, and Matterport3D across 3, 6, and 9 input views, with up to +17.8% PSNR improvement over the current SOTA baseline, requiring no training or fine-tuning of the diffusion model.
AI.conclusion: PanoPlane demonstrates that panoramic scene completion with attention steering can effectively replace unconstrained hallucination with grounded surface extrapolation, enabling high-quality sparse-view novel view synthesis without additional model training.

title: TeDiO: Temporal Diagonal Optimization for Training-Free Coherent Video Diffusion
comment: CVPR'26 Workshop on Agentic AI for Visual Media
summary: Recent text-to-video diffusion transformers generate visually compelling frames, yet still struggle with temporal coherence, often producing flickering, drifting, or unstable motion. We show that these failures leave a clear imprint inside the model: incoherent videos consistently exhibit irregular, fragmented temporal diagonals in their intermediate self-attention maps, whereas stable motion corresponds to smooth, band-diagonal patterns. Building on this observation, we introduce TeDiO, a training-free, inference-time method that reinforces temporal consistency by regularizing these internal attention patterns. TeDiO estimates diagonal smoothness, identifies unstable regions, and performs lightweight latent updates that promote coherent frame-to-frame dynamics, without modifying model weights or using external motion supervision. Across multiple video diffusion models (e.g., Wan2.1, CogVideoX), TeDiO delivers markedly smoother motion while preserving per-frame visual quality, offering an efficient plug-and-play approach to improving dynamic realism in modern video generation systems.

AI.tldr: TeDiO is a training-free method that improves temporal coherence in text-to-video diffusion transformers by detecting and regularizing irregular self-attention patterns during inference, resulting in smoother motion without quality loss.
AI.motivation: Text-to-video diffusion transformers produce visually appealing frames but suffer from temporal incoherence (flickering, drifting, unstable motion), limiting their dynamic realism.
AI.method: TeDiO diagnoses temporal incoherence via irregular/fragmented temporal diagonals in intermediate self-attention maps (vs. smooth band-diagonal patterns for stable motion), then regularizes these patterns during inference by estimating diagonal smoothness, identifying unstable regions, and performing lightweight latent updates—all without training or external motion supervision.
AI.result: Across multiple video diffusion models (Wan2.1, CogVideoX), TeDiO achieves markedly smoother motion while preserving per-frame visual quality.
AI.conclusion: TeDiO provides an efficient, plug-and-play approach to improving dynamic realism in modern video generation systems without modifying model weights.

title: Rethinking the Good Enough Embedding for Easy Few-Shot Learning
summary: The field of deep visual recognition is undergoing a paradigm shift toward universal representations. The Platonic Representation Hypothesis suggests that diverse architectures trained on massive datasets are converging toward a shared, "ideal" latent space. This again raises a critical question: is a "Good Embedding All You Need?" In this paper, we leverage this convergence to demonstrate that off-the-shelf embeddings are inherently "good enough" for complex tasks, rendering intensive task-specific fine-tuning unnecessary. We explore this hypothesis within the few-shot learning framework, proposing a straightforward, non-parametric pipeline that entirely bypasses backpropagation. By utilizing a k-Nearest Neighbor classifier on frozen DINOv2-L features, we conduct a layer-wise characterization to identify an optimal feature extraction. We further demonstrate that manifold refinement via PCA and ICA provides a beneficial regularizing effect. Our results across four major benchmarks demonstrate that our approach consistently surpasses sophisticated meta-learning algorithms, achieving state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that frozen, off-the-shelf embeddings (DINOv2-L) with a simple k-NN classifier can outperform complex meta-learning methods in few-shot learning, suggesting task-specific fine-tuning is unnecessary.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether universal visual representations are "good enough" for complex tasks, eliminating the need for intensive task-specific fine-tuning in few-shot learning.
AI.method: A non-parametric pipeline using frozen DINOv2-L features + k-NN classifier, with layer-wise characterization for optimal feature extraction and manifold refinement via PCA/ICA.
AI.result: The simple pipeline consistently surpasses sophisticated meta-learning algorithms, achieving state-of-the-art performance across four major few-shot learning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Off-the-shelf embeddings are inherently "good enough" for complex tasks, supporting the Platonic Representation Hypothesis and questioning the necessity of fine-tuning.

title: You Only Landmark Once: Lightweight U-Net Face Super Resolution with YOLO-World Landmark Heatmaps
summary: Face image super-resolution aims to recover high-resolution facial images from severely degraded inputs. Under extreme upscaling factors, fine facial details are often lost, making accurate reconstruction challenging. Existing methods typically rely on heavy network architectures, adversarial training schemes, or separate alignment networks, increasing model complexity and computational cost. To address these issues, we propose a lightweight U-Net based-architecture designed to reconstructs $128{ \times }128$ facial images from severely degraded $16{ \times }16$ inputs, achieving an $8 \times $ magnification. A key contribution is a novel auxiliary-training-free supervision strategy that leverages heatmaps generated by YOLO-World, an open-vocabulary object detector, to localize key facial features such as eyes, nose, and mouth. These heatmaps are converted into spatial weights to form a heatmap-guided loss that emphasizes reconstruction errors in semantically important regions. Unlike prior methods that require dedicated landmark or alignment networks, our approach directly reuses detector outputs as supervision, maintaining an efficient training and inference pipeline. Experiments on the aligned CelebA dataset demonstrate that the proposed loss consistently improves quantitative metrics and produces sharper, more realistic reconstructions. Overall, our results show that lightweight networks can effectively exploit detection-driven priors for perceptually convincing extreme upscaling, without adversarial training or increased computational cost.

AI.tldr: Proposes a lightweight U-Net for 8x face super-resolution (16x16 to 128x128) using a novel heatmap-guided loss from YOLO-World detections, eliminating the need for auxiliary networks or adversarial training.
AI.motivation: Existing face SR methods rely on heavy networks, adversarial training, or separate alignment networks, increasing complexity and computational cost. The goal is to achieve high-quality extreme upscaling with a lightweight, efficient pipeline.
AI.method: A lightweight U-Net reconstructs 128x128 faces from 16x16 inputs. A novel auxiliary-training-free supervision uses YOLO-World to generate heatmaps of facial features (eyes, nose, mouth), converted into spatial weights for a heatmap-guided loss that emphasizes semantically important regions.
AI.result: Experiments on aligned CelebA show the proposed loss consistently improves quantitative metrics and produces sharper, more realistic reconstructions, achieving perceptually convincing results without adversarial training or increased cost.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight networks can effectively exploit detection-driven priors for extreme face super-resolution, eliminating the need for complex architectures or adversarial training.

title: CoReDiT: Spatial Coherence-Guided Token Pruning and Reconstruction for Efficient Diffusion Transformers
summary: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) deliver remarkable image and video generation quality but incur high computational cost, limiting scalability and on-device deployment. We introduce CoReDiT, a structured token pruning framework for DiTs across vision tasks. CoReDiT uses a linear-time spatial coherence score to estimate local redundancy in the latent token lattice and skips high coherence (redundant) tokens in self-attention. To maintain a dense representation and avoid visual discontinuities, we reconstruct skipped attention outputs via coherence-guided aggregation of spatially neighboring retained tokens. We further introduce a progressive, block-adaptive pruning schedule that increases pruning gradually and allocates larger budgets to blocks and denoising steps with higher redundancy. Across state-of-the-art diffusion backbones including PixArt-α and MagicDrive-V2, CoReDiT achieves up to 55% self-attention FLOPs reduction and inference speedups of 1.33x on cloud GPUs and 1.72x on mobile NPUs, while maintaining high visual quality. Notably, CoReDiT also increases on-device memory head-room, enabling higher-resolution generation.

AI.tldr: CoReDiT is a token pruning framework for Diffusion Transformers that reduces computational costs by skipping redundant tokens in self-attention using coherence scores, achieving up to 55% FLOPs reduction and 1.33-1.72x speedup on various hardware while maintaining visual quality.
AI.motivation: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) deliver high-quality image/video generation but suffer from high computational cost, limiting scalability and on-device deployment.
AI.method: CoReDiT uses (1) a linear-time spatial coherence score to identify redundant tokens, (2) coherence-guided aggregation to reconstruct skipped attention outputs from neighboring retained tokens, and (3) a progressive block-adaptive pruning schedule that allocates larger budgets to more redundant blocks and denoising steps.
AI.result: CoReDiT achieves up to 55% self-attention FLOPs reduction, 1.33x speedup on cloud GPUs, 1.72x on mobile NPUs, and increases on-device memory headroom for higher-resolution generation across PixArt-α and MagicDrive-V2.
AI.conclusion: CoReDiT effectively reduces computational cost of DiTs across vision tasks while maintaining visual quality, enabling better scalability and on-device deployment including higher-resolution generation.

title: Automatic Landmark-Based Segmentation of Human Subcortical Structures in MRI
comment: 7 pages, 5 figures. Accepted for presentation at the 48th Annual International Conference of the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society (EMBC 2026)
summary: Precise segmentation of brain structures in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is essential for reliable neuroimaging analysis, yet voxel-wise deep models often yield anatomically inconsistent results that diverge from expert-defined boundaries. In this research, we propose a landmark-guided 3D brain segmentation approach that explicitly mimics the manual segmentation protocol of the Harvard--Oxford Atlas. A Global-to-Local network automatically detects 16 landmarks representing key subcortical reference points. Then, a semantic segmentation model produces a coarse segmentation of 12 anatomical labels, each grouping multiple subcortical regions. Finally, a landmark-driven post-processing step separates these 12 labels into 26 distinct structures by enforcing local anatomical constraints. Experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements in boundary accuracy. Overall, integrating learned landmarks aligns segmentations more closely with manual protocols.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a landmark-guided 3D brain segmentation approach that mimics manual segmentation protocols by detecting 16 anatomical landmarks, producing coarse segmentation of 12 labels, and then refining into 26 distinct structures via landmark-driven post-processing, achieving better boundary accuracy.
AI.motivation: Voxel-wise deep models often produce anatomically inconsistent segmentation results that diverge from expert-defined boundaries in brain MRI analysis.
AI.method: A Global-to-Local network detects 16 landmarks; a semantic segmentation model produces 12 coarse anatomical labels; landmark-driven post-processing separates them into 26 distinct structures using local anatomical constraints.
AI.result: The method achieves consistent improvements in boundary accuracy compared to baseline voxel-wise deep models.
AI.conclusion: Integrating learned landmarks aligns segmentations more closely with manual protocols, improving anatomical consistency.

title: Implicit spatial-frequency fusion of hyperspectral and lidar data via kolmogorov-arnold networks
summary:
Hyperspectral image (HSI) classification is challenging in complex scenes due to spectral ambiguity, spatial heterogeneity, and the strong coupling between material properties and geometric structures. Although LiDAR provides complementary elevation information, most HSI-LiDAR fusion methods rely on CNNs or MLPs with fixed activation functions and linear weights. These methods struggle to model structural discontinuities in LiDAR data, intricate spectral features of HSI, and their interactions. In addition, fusion of the two modalities in both spatial and frequency domains with LiDAR guidance remains underexplored.
  To address these issues, we propose the Implicit Frequency-Geometry Fusion Network (IFGNet), which leverages Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) with learnable spline-based functions to adaptively capture highly nonlinear relationships between hyperspectral and LiDAR features. Furthermore, IFGNet introduces a LiDAR-guided implicit aggregation module in both spatial and frequency domains, enhancing geometry-aware spatial representations while capturing global structural patterns.
  Experiments on the Houston 2013 and MUUFL benchmarks demonstrate that IFGNet consistently outperforms existing fusion methods in overall accuracy, average accuracy, and Cohen's Kappa, while maintaining an efficient architecture.

AI.tldr: IFGNet uses Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks and LiDAR-guided frequency-spatial fusion to improve HSI classification, outperforming existing methods on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: HSI classification faces challenges from spectral ambiguity, spatial heterogeneity, and complex material-geometry coupling. Existing HSI-LiDAR fusion methods using CNNs/MLPs with fixed activation functions fail to model structural discontinuities, intricate spectral features, and their interactions. Additionally, fusion in both spatial and frequency domains with LiDAR guidance is underexplored.
AI.method: Proposes IFGNet, which uses Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) with learnable spline-based functions to capture nonlinear HSI-LiDAR relationships. Introduces a LiDAR-guided implicit aggregation module operating in both spatial and frequency domains for geometry-aware representations and global structural patterns.
AI.result: Outperforms existing fusion methods on Houston 2013 and MUUFL benchmarks in overall accuracy, average accuracy, and Cohen's Kappa, with efficient architecture.
AI.conclusion: Learnable spline-based KANs and dual-domain LiDAR-guided aggregation effectively address limitations of fixed-activation fusion methods for HSI-LiDAR classification.

title: Generative Deep Learning for Computational Destaining and Restaining of Unregistered Digital Pathology Images
summary: Conditional generative adversarial networks (cGANs) have enabled high-fidelity computational staining and destaining of hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) in digital pathology whole-slide images (WSI). However, their ability to generalize to out-of-distribution WSI across institutions without retraining remains insufficiently characterized. Previously developed cGAN models trained on 102 registered prostate core biopsy WSIs from Brigham and Women's Hospital were evaluated on 82 spatially unregistered WSIs acquired at Stanford University. To mitigate domain shift without retraining, a preprocessing pipeline consisting of histogram-based stain normalization for H&E-stained WSIs and channel-wise intensity calibration for unstained WSIs was developed. Because image registration was intentionally omitted for real-world deployment conditions, the reported quantitative results are conservative lower bounds reflecting both model performance and limited spatial alignment. Under these conditions, virtual destaining achieved a Pearson correlation coefficient (PCC) of 0.854, structural similarity index measure (SSIM) of 0.699, and peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) of 18.41 dB. H&E restaining from computationally destained outputs outperformed direct staining from ground-truth unstained inputs across all metrics (PCC: 0.798 vs. 0.715; SSIM: 0.756 vs. 0.718; PSNR: 20.08 vs. 18.51 dB), suggesting that preprocessing quality may be more limiting than model capacity. Qualitative pathological review indicated preservation of benign glandular structures while showing that malignant glands were often rendered with vessel-like morphologies. These findings support the feasibility of applying cGAN-based computational H&E staining and destaining generative models to external WSI datasets using preprocessing-based adaptation alone while defining specific morphological targets for future domain adaptation.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates cGAN-based computational H&E staining/destaining models on out-of-distribution whole-slide images across institutions using only preprocessing-based adaptation (histogram normalization and intensity calibration) without retraining. Virtual destaining achieved PCC 0.854, SSIM 0.699, PSNR 18.41 dB, and restaining outperformed direct staining, though malignant glands showed vessel-like morphologies.
AI.motivation: To characterize whether cGAN-based computational staining/destaining models can generalize to out-of-distribution whole-slide images from different institutions without retraining, addressing the insufficiently characterized domain shift problem in digital pathology.
AI.method: Evaluated previously trained cGAN models (trained on 102 registered prostate core biopsy WSIs from Brigham and Women's Hospital) on 82 spatially unregistered WSIs from Stanford University, using a preprocessing pipeline of histogram-based stain normalization (H&E) and channel-wise intensity calibration (unstained), intentionally omitting image registration for real-world deployment.
AI.result: Virtual destaining: PCC 0.854, SSIM 0.699, PSNR 18.41 dB. H&E restaining from destained outputs outperformed direct staining from ground-truth unstained (PCC: 0.798 vs 0.715; SSIM: 0.756 vs 0.718; PSNR: 20.08 vs 18.51 dB). Pathological review showed preserved benign glands but malignant glands rendered with vessel-like morphologies.
AI.conclusion: Preprocessing-based adaptation alone enables feasible application of cGAN-based computational staining/destaining to external WSI datasets without retraining, though domain adaptation is still needed for malignant tissue morphology. Preprocessing quality may be more limiting than model capacity.

title: Towards Real-Time Autonomous Navigation: Transformer-Based Catheter Tip Tracking in Fluoroscopy
comment: Harry Robertshaw and Yanghe Hao contributed equally to this work. Published in the International Journal of Computer Assisted Radiology and Surgery
summary: Purpose: Mechanical thrombectomy (MT) improves stroke outcomes, but is limited by a lack of local treatment access. Widespread distribution of reinforcement learning (RL)-based robotic systems can be used to alleviate this challenge through autonomous navigation, but current RL methods require live device tip coordinate tracking to function. This paper aims to develop and evaluate a real-time catheter tip tracking pipeline under fluoroscopy, addressing challenges such as low contrast, noise, and device occlusion. Methods: A multi-threaded pipeline was designed, incorporating frame reading, preprocessing, inference, and post-processing. Deep learning segmentation models, including U-Net, U-Net+Transformer, and SegFormer, were trained and benchmarked using two-class and three-class formulations. Post-processing involved two-step component filtering, one-pixel medial skeletonization, and greedy arc-length path following with contour fall-back. Results: On manually-labeled moderate complexity fluoroscopic video data, the two-class SegFormer achieved a mean absolute error of 4.44 mm, outperforming U-Net (4.60 mm), U-Net+Transformer (6.20 mm) and all three-class models (5.19-7.74 mm). On segmentation benchmarks, the system exceeded state-of-the-art CathAction results with improvements of up to +5% in Dice scores for three-segmentation. Conclusion: The results demonstrate that the proposed multi-threaded tracking framework maintains stable performance under challenging imaging conditions, outperforming prior benchmarks, while providing a reliable and efficient foundation for RL-based autonomous MT navigation.

AI.tldr: A real-time catheter tip tracking pipeline using deep learning segmentation models (SegFormer, U-Net) was developed for fluoroscopy to enable RL-based autonomous mechanical thrombectomy navigation, achieving 4.44 mm MAE and outperforming prior benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Mechanical thrombectomy improves stroke outcomes but is limited by lack of local access; RL-based robotic systems could help but require real-time catheter tip tracking under challenging fluoroscopic conditions (low contrast, noise, occlusion).
AI.method: A multi-threaded pipeline with frame reading, preprocessing, inference, and post-processing was designed. Deep learning models (U-Net, U-Net+Transformer, SegFormer) were trained with two-class and three-class formulations. Post-processing used component filtering, medial skeletonization, and greedy arc-length path following with contour fall-back.
AI.result: Two-class SegFormer achieved 4.44 mm MAE, outperforming U-Net (4.60 mm), U-Net+Transformer (6.20 mm), and all three-class models (5.19-7.74 mm). The system improved state-of-the-art CathAction Dice scores by up to +5%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed tracking framework provides stable, efficient performance under challenging imaging conditions, offering a reliable foundation for RL-based autonomous MT navigation.

title: Image Restoration via Diffusion Models with Dynamic Resolution
summary: Diffusion models (DMs) have exhibited remarkable efficacy in various image restoration tasks. However, existing approaches typically operate within the high-dimensional pixel space, resulting in high computational overhead. While methods based on latent DMs seek to alleviate this issue by utilizing the compressed latent space of a variational autoencoder, they require repeated encoder-decoder inference. This introduces significant additional computational burdens, often resulting in runtime performance that is even inferior to that of their pixel-space counterparts. To mitigate the computational inefficiency, this work proposes projecting data into lower-dimensional subspaces using dynamic resolution DMs to accelerate the inference process. We first fine-tune pre-trained DMs for dynamic resolution priors and adapt DPS and DAPS, which are two widely used pixel-space methods for general image restoration tasks, into the proposed framework, yielding methods we refer to as SubDPS and SubDAPS, respectively. Given the favorable inference speed and reconstruction fidelity of SubDAPS, we introduce an enhanced variant termed SubDAPS++ to further boost both reconstruction efficiency and quality. Empirical evaluations across diverse image datasets and various restoration tasks demonstrate that the proposed methods outperform recent DM-based approaches in the majority of experimental scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/StarNextDay/SubDAPS.git.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes SubDPS and SubDAPS, which accelerate diffusion model-based image restoration by projecting data into lower-dimensional subspaces using dynamic resolution priors, outperforming prior DM-based methods in efficiency and quality.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion models for image restoration suffer from high computational overhead due to operating in high-dimensional pixel space, while latent DM approaches add extra encoder-decoder inference burdens that can be even slower than pixel-space methods.
AI.method: The authors fine-tune pre-trained diffusion models with dynamic resolution priors and adapt DPS/DAPS into a subspace framework (SubDPS, SubDAPS), plus an enhanced variant SubDAPS++ that improves both reconstruction efficiency and quality.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across diverse datasets and restoration tasks show the proposed methods outperform recent DM-based approaches in most experimental scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Projecting data into lower-dimensional subspaces via dynamic resolution DMs effectively mitigates computational inefficiency while maintaining or improving reconstruction fidelity.

title: PhyMotion: Structured 3D Motion Reward for Physics-Grounded Human Video Generation
comment: First two authors contributed equally, website: https://phy-motion.github.io/
summary: Generating realistic human motion is a central yet unsolved challenge in video generation. While reinforcement learning (RL)-based post-training has driven recent gains in general video quality, extending it to human motion remains bottlenecked by a reward signal that cannot reliably score motion realism. Existing video rewards primarily rely on 2D perceptual signals, without explicitly modeling the 3D body state, contact, and dynamics underlying articulated human motion, and often assign high scores to videos with floating bodies or physically implausible movements. To address this, we propose PhyMotion, a structured, fine-grained motion reward that grounds recovered 3D human trajectories in a physics simulator and evaluates motion quality along multiple dimensions of physical feasibility. Concretely, we recover SMPL body meshes from generated videos, retarget them onto a humanoid in the MuJoCo physics simulator, and evaluate the resulting motion along three axes: kinematic plausibility, contact and balance consistency, and dynamic feasibility. Each component provides a continuous and interpretable signal tied to a specific aspect of motion quality, allowing the reward to capture which aspects of motion are physically correct or violated. Experiments show that PhyMotion achieves stronger correlation with human judgments than existing reward formulations. These gains carry over to RL-based post-training, where optimizing PhyMotion leads to larger and more consistent improvements than optimizing existing rewards, improving motion realism across both autoregressive and bidirectional video generators under both automatic metrics and blind human evaluation (+68 Elo gain). Ablations show that the three axes provide complementary supervision signals, while the reward preserves overall video generation quality with only modest training overhead.

AI.tldr: PhyMotion proposes a structured, physics-grounded reward for human motion video generation, using 3D body tracking and physics simulation to evaluate kinematic, contact/balance, and dynamic feasibility, achieving better alignment with human judgment and improving RL-based post-training for video generators.
AI.motivation: Existing video rewards rely on 2D perceptual signals that fail to capture 3D body state, contact, and dynamics, often scoring physically implausible motions (e.g., floating bodies) highly, creating a bottleneck for RL-based post-training of human motion video generation.
AI.method: Recovers SMPL body meshes from generated videos, retargets them onto a humanoid in the MuJoCo physics simulator, and evaluates motion quality along three axes: kinematic plausibility, contact/balance consistency, and dynamic feasibility.
AI.result: Achieves stronger correlation with human judgments than existing rewards; RL-based post-training with PhyMotion yields larger and more consistent improvements (+68 Elo gain) across both autoregressive and bidirectional video generators under automatic metrics and blind human evaluation.
AI.conclusion: PhyMotion provides an effective, multi-dimensional physics-grounded reward for human motion realism, with complementary supervision from its three axes and modest training overhead.

title: Diagnosing and Correcting Concept Omission in Multimodal Diffusion Transformers
summary: Multimodal Diffusion Transformers (MM-DiTs) have achieved remarkable progress in text-to-image generation, yet they frequently suffer from concept omission, where specified objects or attributes fail to emerge in the generated image. By performing linear probing on text tokens, we demonstrate that text embeddings can distinguish a characteristic `omission signal' representing the absence of target concepts. Leveraging this insight, we propose Omission Signal Intervention (OSI), which amplifies the omission signal to actively catalyze the generation of missing concepts. Comprehensive experiments on FLUX.1-Dev and SD3.5-Medium demonstrate that OSI significantly alleviates concept omission even in extreme scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies an "omission signal" in text embeddings that correlates with missing concepts in MM-DiT text-to-image generation, and proposes Omission Signal Intervention (OSI) to amplify this signal and reduce concept omission.
AI.motivation: Multimodal Diffusion Transformers (MM-DiTs) frequently fail to generate specified objects or attributes (concept omission), which limits their reliability in text-to-image generation.
AI.method: The authors perform linear probing on text tokens to identify an "omission signal" representing missing concepts, then propose OSI to amplify this signal during generation.
AI.result: OSI significantly alleviates concept omission in FLUX.1-Dev and SD3.5-Medium, even in extreme scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Amplifying omission signals in text embeddings is an effective approach to reduce concept omission in MM-DiTs.

title: CreFlow: Corrective Reflow for Sparse-Reward Embodied Video Diffusion RL
summary: Video generation models trained on heterogeneous data with likelihood-surrogate objectives can produce visually plausible rollouts that violate physical constraints in embodied manipulation. Although reinforcement-learning post-training offers a natural route to adapting VGMs, existing video-RL rewards often reduce each rollout to a low-level visual metric, whereas manipulation video evaluation requires logic-based verification of whether the rollout satisfies a compositional task specification. To fill this gap, we introduce a compositional constraint-based reward model for post-training embodied video generation models, which automatically formulates task requirements as a composition of Linear Temporal Logic constraints, providing faithful rewards and localized error information in generated videos. To achieve effective improvement in high-dimensional video generation using these reward signals, we further propose CreFlow, a novel online RL framework with two key designs: i) a credit-aware NFT loss that confines the RL update to reward-relevant regions, preventing perturbations to unrelated regions during post-training; and ii) a corrective reflow loss that leverages within-group positive samples as an explicit estimate of the correction direction, stabilizing and accelerating training. Experiments show that CreFlow yields reward judgments better aligned with human and simulator success labels than existing methods and improves downstream execution success by 23.8 percentage points across eight bimanual manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: CreFlow is an online RL framework for post-training video generation models in embodied manipulation. It uses compositional Linear Temporal Logic constraints for reward modeling and introduces a credit-aware NFT loss and corrective reflow loss to improve video generation quality, achieving 23.8% improvement in downstream manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing video generation models produce visually plausible but physically invalid rollouts for embodied manipulation. Current video-RL rewards rely on low-level visual metrics rather than logic-based verification of compositional task specifications, creating a gap for manipulation video evaluation.
AI.method: The paper proposes (1) a compositional constraint-based reward model that formulates task requirements as Linear Temporal Logic constraints, and (2) CreFlow, an online RL framework with credit-aware NFT loss (confines updates to reward-relevant regions) and corrective reflow loss (uses positive samples for correction direction estimation).
AI.result: CreFlow achieves reward judgments better aligned with human and simulator success labels, and improves downstream execution success by 23.8 percentage points across eight bimanual manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed compositional constraint-based reward model and CreFlow framework effectively address the physical validity gap in video generation for embodied manipulation by enabling logic-verifiable rewards and stable RL-based post-training.

title: KVPO: ODE-Native GRPO for Autoregressive Video Alignment via KV Semantic Exploration
summary: Aligning streaming autoregressive (AR) video generators with human preferences is challenging. Existing reinforcement learning methods predominantly rely on noise-based exploration and SDE-based surrogate policies that are mismatched to the deterministic ODE dynamics of distilled AR models, and tend to perturb low-level appearance rather than the high-level semantic storyline progression critical for long-horizon coherence. To address these limitations, we present KVPO, an ODE-native online Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) framework for aligning streaming video generators. For diversity exploration, KVPO introduces a causal-semantic exploration paradigm that relocates the source of variation from stochastic noise to the historical KV cache. By stochastically routing historical KV entries, it constructs semantically diverse generation branches that remain strictly on the data manifold. For policy modeling, KVPO introduces a velocity-field surrogate policy based on Trajectory Velocity Energy (TVE), which quantifies branch likelihood in flow-matching velocity space and yields a reward-weighted contrastive objective fully consistent with the native ODE formulation. Experiments on multiple distilled AR video generators demonstrate consistent gains in visual quality, motion quality, and text-video alignment across both single-prompt short-video and multi-prompt long-video settings.

AI.tldr: KVPO presents an ODE-native GRPO framework that aligns streaming AR video generators with human preferences using causal-semantic exploration (via KV cache routing) and velocity-field surrogate policy (via Trajectory Velocity Energy), achieving consistent improvements across short and long video generation.
AI.motivation: Existing RL methods for aligning streaming AR video generators rely on noise-based exploration and SDE-based surrogate policies mismatched to the deterministic ODE dynamics of distilled AR models. These approaches perturb low-level appearance rather than high-level semantic storyline progression, hindering long-horizon coherence.
AI.method: KVPO introduces (1) causal-semantic exploration: relocates variation from stochastic noise to historical KV cache by stochastically routing historical KV entries, constructing semantically diverse branches on the data manifold; (2) velocity-field surrogate policy based on Trajectory Velocity Energy (TVE), which quantifies branch likelihood in flow-matching velocity space, yielding a reward-weighted contrastive objective consistent with ODE formulation.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple distilled AR video generators show consistent gains in visual quality, motion quality, and text-video alignment for both single-prompt short-video and multi-prompt long-video settings.
AI.conclusion: KVPO effectively addresses the mismatch between existing RL alignment methods and ODE dynamics of distilled AR video generators by proposing ODE-native exploration (KV cache routing) and policy modeling (TVE-based surrogate), leading to better semantic-level alignment and long-horizon coherence.

title: ICED: Concept-level Machine Unlearning via Interpretable Concept Decomposition
summary: Machine unlearning in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) is typically performed at the image or instance level, making it difficult to precisely remove target knowledge without affecting unrelated semantics. This issue is especially pronounced since a single image often contains multiple entangled concepts, including both target concepts to be forgotten and contextual information that should be preserved. In this paper, we propose an interpretable concept-level unlearning framework for VLMs, which constructs a compact task-specific concept vocabulary from the forgetting set using a multimodal large language model. In addition to modality alignment, visual representations are decomposed into sparse, nonnegative combinations of semantic concepts, providing an explicit interface for fine-grained knowledge manipulation. Based on this decomposition, our method formulates unlearning as concept-level optimization, where target concepts are selectively suppressed while intra-instance non-target semantics and global cross-modal knowledge are preserved. Extensive experiments across both in-domain and out-of-domain forgetting settings demonstrate that our method enables more comprehensive target forgetting, better preserves non-target knowledge within the same image, and maintains competitive model utility compared with existing VLM unlearning methods.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an interpretable concept-level unlearning framework for Vision-Language Models that decomposes visual representations into sparse, nonnegative concept combinations, enabling precise removal of target concepts while preserving non-target semantics within the same image.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM unlearning methods operate at the image/instance level, making it difficult to precisely remove target knowledge without affecting unrelated semantics, as a single image often contains entangled target and non-target concepts.
AI.method: 1) Constructs a compact task-specific concept vocabulary from the forgetting set using a multimodal large language model. 2) Decomposes visual representations into sparse, nonnegative combinations of semantic concepts. 3) Formulates unlearning as concept-level optimization, selectively suppressing target concepts while preserving intra-instance non-target semantics and global cross-modal knowledge.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on both in-domain and out-of-domain forgetting settings show the method achieves more comprehensive target forgetting, better preserves non-target knowledge within the same image, and maintains competitive model utility compared to existing VLM unlearning methods.
AI.conclusion: The concept-level unlearning framework provides finer-grained knowledge manipulation for VLMs, effectively addressing the entanglement issue in instance-level unlearning by leveraging interpretable concept decomposition.

title: CoRDS: Coreset-based Representative and Diverse Selection for Streaming Video Understanding
summary: Streaming video understanding with large vision-language models (VLMs) requires a compact memory that can support future reasoning over an ever-growing visual history. A common solution is to compress the key-value (KV) cache, but existing streaming methods typically rely on local token-wise heuristics, such as recency, temporal redundancy, or saliency, which do not explicitly optimize whether the retained cache is representative of the accumulated history. We propose to view KV-cache compression as a coreset selection problem: rather than scoring tokens independently for retention, we select a small subset that covers the geometry of the accumulated visual cache. Our method operates in a joint KV representation and introduces a bicriteria objective that balances coverage in key and value spaces, preserving both retrieval structure and output-relevant information. To encourage a more diverse retained subset, we further introduce an orthogonality-driven diversity criterion that favors candidates contributing new directions beyond the current selection, and connect this criterion to log-determinant subset selection. Across four open-source VLMs and five long-video and streaming-video benchmarks, our method improves over heuristic streaming compression baselines under a fixed cache budget. These results highlight that representative coreset selection offers a more effective principle, than token-wise pruning, for memory-constrained streaming video understanding.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a coreset selection approach for KV-cache compression in streaming video understanding with VLMs, which selects a compact subset that covers the geometry of the visual cache rather than using token-wise heuristics, achieving better performance across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing streaming methods for video understanding with VLMs rely on local token-wise heuristics (recency, redundancy, saliency) for KV-cache compression, which do not explicitly optimize whether the retained cache is representative of the accumulated history.
AI.method: View KV-cache compression as a coreset selection problem with a bicriteria objective balancing coverage in key and value spaces, plus an orthogonality-driven diversity criterion connected to log-determinant subset selection.
AI.result: Across four open-source VLMs and five long-video/streaming-video benchmarks, the method improves over heuristic streaming compression baselines under a fixed cache budget.
AI.conclusion: Representative coreset selection is a more effective principle than token-wise pruning for memory-constrained streaming video understanding.

title: TurboVGGT: Fast Visual Geometry Reconstruction with Adaptive Alternating Attention
summary: Recent feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods, such as visual geometry transformers, have substantially advanced the traditional per-scene optimization paradigm by enabling effective multi-view reconstruction in a single forward pass. However, most existing methods struggle to achieve a balance between reconstruction quality and computational efficiency, which limits their scalability and efficiency. Although some efficient visual geometry transformers have recently emerged, they typically use the same sparsity ratio across layers and frames and lack mechanisms to adaptively learn representative tokens to capture global relationships, leading to suboptimal performance. In this work, we propose TurboVGGT, a novel approach that employs an efficient visual geometry transformer with adaptive alternating attention for fast multi-view 3D reconstruction. Specifically, TurboVGGT employs an end-to-end trainable framework with adaptive sparse global attention guided by adaptive sparsity selection to capture global relationships across frames and frame attention to aggregate local details within each frame. In the adaptive sparse global attention, TurboVGGT adaptively learns representative tokens with varying sparsity levels for global geometry modeling, considering that token importance varies across frames, attention layers operate tokens at different levels of abstraction, and global dependencies rely on structurally informative regions. Extensive experiments on multiple 3D reconstruction benchmarks demonstrate that TurboVGGT achieves fast multi-view reconstruction while maintaining competitive reconstruction quality compared with state-of-the-art methods. Project page: https://turbovggt.github.io/.

AI.tldr: TurboVGGT proposes an efficient visual geometry transformer with adaptive alternating attention for fast multi-view 3D reconstruction, balancing quality and computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods struggle to balance reconstruction quality and computational efficiency, and they often use fixed sparsity ratios and lack adaptive token learning for capturing global relationships.
AI.method: TurboVGGT uses an end-to-end framework with adaptive sparse global attention (guided by adaptive sparsity selection to learn representative tokens) for cross-frame global relationships, and frame attention for local detail aggregation within each frame.
AI.result: TurboVGGT achieves fast multi-view reconstruction with competitive quality compared to state-of-the-art methods across multiple 3D reconstruction benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive alternating attention with varying sparsity levels effectively balances efficiency and quality for multi-view 3D reconstruction.

title: D2-CDIG: Controlled Diffusion Remote Sensing Image Generation with Dual Priors of DEM and Cloud-Fog
