Federated learning (FedL) provides a decentralized alternative; however, existing FedL and federated DG (FedDG) approaches rely almost exclusively on low-order statistics, assuming Gaussian-like stain distributions. In contrast, real-world staining processes often produce asymmetric, heavy-tailed color distributions due to biochemical diffusion and scanner nonlinearity. Consequently, current methods fail to model the higher-order, non-Gaussian characteristics dominating real-world stain variability. To address this, we propose FedStain, a stain-aware FedDG framework explicitly incorporating higher-order stain moments--skewness and kurtosis--as compact statistical descriptors exchanged during federated optimization. These descriptors require no pixel-level data transmission, preserving strict privacy and communication efficiency, while enabling the global model to capture stain variability missed by low-order statistics. FedStain also employs a contrastive, cross-site parameter aggregation strategy to promote stain-invariant representations without relaxing data constraints. Extensive experiments on Camelyon17 and our new MvMidog-Fed benchmark show FedStain yields consistent improvements, outperforming state-of-the-art FedL, DG, and FedDG baselines by up to +3.9% absolute accuracy. To our knowledge, FedStain is the first FedDG approach to explicitly model higher-order stain statistics, enabling robust cross-institutional deployment in computational pathology.

AI.tldr: FedStain is a federated domain generalization framework for whole-slide image analysis that explicitly models higher-order stain statistics (skewness and kurtosis) to handle non-Gaussian, heavy-tailed stain distributions across institutions, achieving up to +3.9% accuracy improvement without violating data privacy.
AI.motivation: Existing federated DG methods rely on low-order statistics assuming Gaussian-like stain distributions, but real-world staining processes produce asymmetric, heavy-tailed color distributions due to biochemical diffusion and scanner nonlinearity. This mismatch causes current methods to miss higher-order, non-Gaussian characteristics dominating real-world stain variability.
AI.method: FedStain explicitly incorporates higher-order stain moments (skewness and kurtosis) as compact statistical descriptors exchanged during federated optimization, requiring no pixel-level data transmission. It also employs a contrastive, cross-site parameter aggregation strategy to promote stain-invariant representations.
AI.result: On Camelyon17 and the new MvMidog-Fed benchmark, FedStain outperforms state-of-the-art FedL, DG, and FedDG baselines by up to +3.9% absolute accuracy.
AI.conclusion: FedStain is the first FedDG approach to explicitly model higher-order stain statistics, enabling robust cross-institutional deployment in computational pathology under strict data governance.

title: TOPOS: High-Fidelity and Efficient Industry-Grade 3D Head Generation
summary: High-fidelity 3D head generation plays a crucial role in the film, animation and video game industries. In industrial pipelines, studios typically enforce a fixed reference topology across all head assets, as such a clean and uniform topology is a prerequisite for production-level rigging, skinning and animation. In this paper, we present TOPOS, a framework tailored for single image conditioned 3D head generation that jointly recovers geometry and appearance under such an industry-standard topology. In contrast to general 3D generative models which produce triangle meshes with inconsistent topology and numerous vertices, hindering semantic correspondence and asset-level reuse, TOPOS generates head meshes with a fixed, studio-style topology, enabling consistent vertex-level correspondence across all generated heads. To model heads under this unified topology, we proposed a novel variational autoencoder structure, termed TOPOS-VAE. Inspired by multi-model large language models (MLLMs), our TOPOS-VAE leverages the Perceiver Resampler to convert input pointclouds sampled from head meshes of diverse topologies into the target reference topology. Building upon TOPOS-VAE's structured latent space, we train a rectified flow transformer, TOPOS-DiT, to efficiently generate high-fidelity head meshes from a single image. We further present TOPOS-Texture, an end-to-end module that produces relightable UV texture maps from the same portrait image via fine-tuning a multimodal image generative model. The generated textures are spatially aligned with the underlying mesh geometry and faithfully preserve high-frequency appearance details. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TOPOS achieves state-of-the-art performance on 3D head generation, surpassing both classical face reconstruction methods and general 3D object generative models, highlighting its effectiveness for digital human creation.

AI.tldr: TOPOS is a framework for single-image 3D head generation that produces meshes with fixed, studio-standard topology and relightable UV textures, outperforming existing methods in digital human creation.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D head generation methods produce inconsistent, non-uniform topologies unsuitable for production-level rigging, skinning, and animation. Studios require fixed reference topology across all head assets for industrial pipelines.
AI.method: TOPOS introduces (1) TOPOS-VAE, a variational autoencoder using Perceiver Resampler (inspired by MLLMs) to convert diverse-topology point clouds into a unified reference topology; (2) TOPOS-DiT, a rectified flow transformer for efficient single-image head generation; (3) TOPOS-Texture, an end-to-end module fine-tuning a multimodal image generative model to produce relightable UV texture maps aligned with the fixed mesh geometry.
AI.result: TOPOS achieves state-of-the-art performance on 3D head generation, surpassing classical face reconstruction methods and general 3D generative models, with consistent vertex-level correspondence and high-fidelity appearance details.
AI.conclusion: TOPOS demonstrates that enforcing industry-standard fixed topology in 3D head generation is feasible and beneficial, enabling semantic correspondence and asset-level reuse for digital human creation in production pipelines.

title: VMU-Diff: A Coarse-to-fine Multi-source Data Fusion Framework for Precipitation Nowcasting
summary: Precipitation nowcasting is a vital spatio-temporal prediction task for meteorological applications but faces challenges due to the chaotic property of precipitation systems. Existing methods predominantly rely on single-source radar data to build either deterministic or probabilistic models for extrapolation. However, the single deterministic model suffers from blurring due to MSE convergence. The single probabilistic model, typically represented by diffusion models, can generate fine details but suffers from spurious artifacts that compromise accuracy and computational inefficiency. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel coarse-to-fine Vision Mamba Unet and residual Diffusion (VMU-Diff) based precipitation nowcasting framework. It realizes precipitation nowcasting through a two-stage process, i.e., a deterministic model-based coarse stage to predict global motion trends and a probabilistic model-based fine stage to generate fine prediction details. In the coarse prediction stage, rather than single-source radar data, both radar and multi-band satellite data are taken as input. A spatial-temporal attention block and several Vision mamba state-space blocks realize multi-source data fusion, and predict the future echo global dynamics. The fine-grained stage is realized by a spatio-temporal refine generator based on residual conditional diffusion models. It first obtains spatio-temporal residual features based on coarse prediction and ground truth, and further reconstructs the residual via conditional Mamba state-space module. Experiments on Jiangsu SWAN datasets demonstrate the improvements of our method over state-of-the-art methods, particularly in short-term forecasts.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes VMU-Diff, a two-stage coarse-to-fine precipitation nowcasting framework combining Vision Mamba UNet for deterministic coarse prediction of global motion trends (using radar and multi-band satellite data) and a residual conditional diffusion model for fine-grained detail generation, achieving SOTA results on Jiangsu SWAN datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing precipitation nowcasting methods suffer from either blurry predictions (deterministic models due to MSE convergence) or spurious artifacts and computational inefficiency (probabilistic diffusion models). The paper aims to combine the strengths of both approaches while mitigating their weaknesses.
AI.method: A two-stage coarse-to-fine framework: (1) Coarse stage: A Vision Mamba UNet with spatial-temporal attention and Vision Mamba state-space blocks, taking both radar and multi-band satellite data as input to predict global motion trends. (2) Fine stage: A spatio-temporal refine generator based on residual conditional diffusion models, which extracts spatio-temporal residual features between coarse prediction and ground truth, then reconstructs details via a conditional Mamba state-space module.
AI.result: Experiments on Jiangsu SWAN datasets demonstrate improvements over state-of-the-art methods, particularly in short-term precipitation nowcasting.
AI.conclusion: The coarse-to-fine design effectively addresses the blurring-artifact dilemma by separating global trend prediction and fine detail generation, with multi-source data fusion further enhancing prediction quality.

title: Towards Accurate Single Panoramic 3D Detection: A Semantic Gaussian Centric Approach
comment: Current has been accepted by ICME 2026
summary: Three-dimensional object detection in panoramic imagery is crucial for comprehensive scene understanding, yet accurately mapping 2D features to 3D remains a significant challenge. Prevailing methods often project 2D features onto discrete 3D grids, which break geometric continuity and limit representation efficiency. To overcome this limitation, this paper proposes PanoGSDet, a monocular panoramic 3D detection framework built upon continuous semantic 3D Gaussian representations. The proposed framework comprises a panoramic depth estimation component and a semantic Gaussian component. The panoramic depth estimation component extracts the equirectangular semantic and depth features from the monocular panorama input. The semantic Gaussian component includes a semantic Gaussian lifting module that projects spherical features into 3D semantic Gaussians, a semantic Gaussian optimization module that refines these semantic Gaussians, and a Gaussian guided prediction head that generates 3D bounding boxes from optimized Gaussian representations. Extensive experiments on the Structured3D dataset demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing methods.

AI.tldr: PanoGSDet is a monocular panoramic 3D object detection framework using continuous semantic 3D Gaussian representations instead of discrete 3D grids, achieving state-of-the-art results on the Structured3D dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing methods project 2D features onto discrete 3D grids, which break geometric continuity and limit representation efficiency for panoramic 3D detection.
AI.method: PanoGSDet consists of a panoramic depth estimation component and a semantic Gaussian component, which includes a semantic Gaussian lifting module, a semantic Gaussian optimization module, and a Gaussian guided prediction head for generating 3D bounding boxes.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on the Structured3D dataset show that PanoGSDet significantly outperforms existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Continuous semantic 3D Gaussian representations are an effective alternative to discrete 3D grids for monocular panoramic 3D object detection.

title: MambaRain: Multi-Scale Mamba-Attention Framework for 0-3 Hour Precipitation Nowcasting
summary: Accurate precipitation nowcasting over extended horizons (0-3 hours) is essential for disaster mitigation and operational decision-making, yet remains a critical challenge in the field. Existing deterministic approaches are predominantly constrained to shorter prediction windows (0-2 hours), exhibiting severe performance degradation beyond 90 minutes owing to their inherent difficulty in capturing long-range spatiotemporal dependencies from radar-derived observations. To address these fundamental limitations, we propose MambaRain, a novel multi-scale encoder-decoder architecture that synergistically integrates Mamba's linear-complexity long-range temporal modeling with self-attention mechanisms for explicit spatial correlation capture. The core innovation lies in a hybrid design paradigm wherein Mamba blocks leverage selective state space mechanisms to model global temporal dynamics across extended sequences with computational efficiency, while self-attention modules explicitly characterize spatial correlations within precipitation fields - a capability inherently absent in Mamba's sequential processing paradigm. This complementary synergy enables comprehensive spatiotemporal representation learning, effectively extending the viable forecasting horizon to 2-3 hours with substantial accuracy improvements. Furthermore, we introduce a spectral loss formulation to mitigate blurring artifacts characteristic of chaotic precipitation systems, thereby preserving fine-scale motion details critical for nowcasting accuracy. Experimental validation demonstrates that MambaRain substantially outperforms existing deterministic methodologies in 0-3 hour nowcasting tasks, with particularly pronounced performance gains in the challenging 2-3 hour prediction range.

AI.tldr: MambaRain is a hybrid Mamba-self-attention architecture for precipitation nowcasting that extends accurate predictions to 0-3 hours, significantly outperforming existing methods especially in the challenging 2-3 hour range.
AI.motivation: Existing deterministic nowcasting methods are limited to 0-2 hour predictions with severe degradation beyond 90 minutes due to inability to capture long-range spatiotemporal dependencies from radar data.
AI.method: A multi-scale encoder-decoder hybrid architecture combining Mamba blocks (linear-complexity long-range temporal modeling via selective state space mechanisms) with self-attention modules (explicit spatial correlation capture), plus a spectral loss to reduce blurring artifacts.
AI.result: Substantially outperforms existing deterministic methods on 0-3 hour nowcasting, with particularly pronounced gains in the 2-3 hour prediction range.
AI.conclusion: MambaRain effectively extends viable forecasting horizon to 2-3 hours with improved accuracy by synergistically combining Mamba's temporal efficiency with self-attention's spatial capabilities.

title: ViMU: Benchmarking Video Metaphorical Understanding
summary: Any new medium, once it emerges, is used for more than the transmission of overt content alone. The information it carries typically operates on two levels: one is the content directly presented, while the other is the subtext beneath it-the implicit ideas and intentions the creator seeks to convey through the medium. Likewise, since video technologies became widely adopted, video has served not only as a powerful tool for recording and communicating visual information, but also as a vehicle for emotions, attitudes, and social meanings that are often difficult to articulate explicitly. Thus, the true meaning of many videos does not reside solely in what is shown on screen; it is often embedded in context, style of expression, and the viewer's social experience. Some forms of such video subtext are humorous, while others carry irony, mockery, or criticism. These implicit meanings can also be interpreted very differently across cultural backgrounds and social groups. However, most existing video understanding models still focus primarily on literal visual comprehension, such as recognizing objects, actions, or temporal relations, and lack a systematic ability to understand the metaphorical, ironic, and social meanings embedded in videos. To bridge this gap, we introduce ViMU, the first benchmark designed to systematically evaluate the subtext understanding capabilities of frontier models in videos. ViMU assesses whether video understanding models can go beyond literal perception to infer implicit meaning while grounding their interpretations in multimodal evidence and answering both open-ended and multiple-choice questions. Importantly, all questions are designed to be hint-free, ensuring that no key evidence is disclosed to models before answering.

AI.tldr: ViMU is a new benchmark designed to evaluate whether video understanding models can grasp subtext—implicit meanings like irony, humor, and social critique—beyond literal content, using hint-free, multimodal questions.
AI.motivation: Existing video understanding models focus on literal comprehension (objects, actions, temporal relations) and lack systematic ability to interpret metaphorical, ironic, and social meanings embedded in videos.
AI.method: ViMU evaluates frontier models on subtext understanding through open-ended and multiple-choice questions that require multimodal evidence, with all questions designed to be hint-free (no key evidence disclosed beforehand).
AI.result: The paper introduces ViMU as the first benchmark for systematic evaluation of video subtext understanding, but specific experimental results are not detailed in this abstract.
AI.conclusion: Video understanding models need to go beyond literal perception to infer implicit meanings, and ViMU provides a systematic benchmark to bridge this capability gap.

title: Deep Image Segmentation via Discriminant Feature Learning
summary: Accurate image segmentation remains challenging, particularly in generating sharp, confident boundaries. While modern architectures have advanced the field, many of them still rely on standard loss functions like Cross-Entropy and Dice, which often neglect the discriminative structure of learned features, leading to inaccurate boundaries. This work introduces Deep Discriminant Analysis (DDA), a differentiable, architecture-agnostic loss function that embeds classical discriminant principles for network training. DDA explicitly maximizes between-class variance while minimizing within-class one, promoting compact and separable feature distributions without increasing inference cost. Evaluations on the DIS5K benchmark demonstrate that DDA consistently improves segmentation accuracy, boundary sharpness, and model confidence across various architectures. Our results show that integrating discriminant analysis offers a simple, effective path for building more robust segmentation models.

AI.tldr: DDA is a differentiable loss function that improves image segmentation by maximizing between-class variance and minimizing within-class variance, leading to sharper boundaries and better model confidence without added inference cost.
AI.motivation: Standard loss functions like Cross-Entropy and Dice neglect discriminative feature structure, resulting in inaccurate and blurry segmentation boundaries.
AI.method: Deep Discriminant Analysis (DDA) — a differentiable, architecture-agnostic loss function that embeds classical discriminant analysis principles to maximize between-class and minimize within-class variance during training.
AI.result: On the DIS5K benchmark, DDA consistently improves segmentation accuracy, boundary sharpness, and model confidence across various architectures.
AI.conclusion: Integrating discriminant analysis into loss design offers a simple yet effective path toward more robust segmentation models.

title: CalibAnyView: Beyond Single-View Camera Calibration in the Wild
summary: Camera calibration is a fundamental prerequisite for reliable geometric perception, yet classical approaches rely on controlled acquisition setups that are impractical for in-the-wild imagery. Recent learning-based methods have shown promising results for single-view calibration, but inherently neglect geometric consistency across multiple views. We introduce CalibAnyView, a unified formulation that supports an arbitrary number of input views ($N \geq 1$) by explicitly modeling cross-view geometric consistency. To facilitate this, we construct a large-scale multi-view video dataset covering diverse real-world scenarios, including multiple camera models, dynamic scenes, realistic motion trajectories, and heterogeneous lens distortions. Building on this dataset, we develop a multi-view transformer that predicts dense perspective fields, which are further integrated into a geometric optimization framework to jointly estimate camera intrinsics and gravity direction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CalibAnyView consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieves strong robustness under single-view settings, and further improves with multi-view inference, providing a reliable foundation for downstream tasks such as 3D reconstruction and robotic perception in the wild.

AI.tldr: CalibAnyView is a unified camera calibration method that works with any number of input views (N≥1) by modeling cross-view geometric consistency, using a multi-view transformer and geometric optimization to jointly estimate camera intrinsics and gravity direction.
AI.motivation: Classical calibration requires controlled setups impractical for in-the-wild imagery, while learning-based single-view methods neglect geometric consistency across multiple views.
AI.method: (1) Constructs a large-scale multi-view video dataset with diverse real-world scenarios. (2) Develops a multi-view transformer predicting dense perspective fields. (3) Integrates predictions into a geometric optimization framework to jointly estimate camera intrinsics and gravity direction.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods, shows strong robustness in single-view settings, and further improves with multi-view inference.
AI.conclusion: Provides a reliable foundation for downstream tasks such as 3D reconstruction and robotic perception in the wild.

title: Do We Really Need External Tools to Mitigate Hallucinations? SIRA: Shared-Prefix Internal Reconstruction of Attribution
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) often hallucinate when language priors dominate weak or ambiguous visual evidence. Existing contrastive decoding methods mitigate this problem by comparing predictions from the original image with those from externally perturbed visual inputs, but such references can introduce off-manifold artifacts and require costly extra forward passes. We propose SIRA, a training-free internal contrastive decoding framework that constructs a counterfactual reference inside the same LVLM by exploiting the staged information flow of multimodal transformers. Instead of removing visual information from the input, SIRA first lets image and text tokens interact through a shared prefix, forming an aligned multimodal state that preserves prompt interpretation, decoding history, positional structure, and early visual grounding. It then forks a counterfactual branch in later transformer layers, where attention to image-token positions is masked. This branch retains the shared multimodal context but lacks continued access to fine-grained visual evidence, yielding a language-prior-dominated internal reference for token-level contrast. During decoding, SIRA suppresses tokens that remain strong without late visual access and favors predictions whose advantage depends on the full visual pathway. Experiments on POPE, CHAIR, and AMBER with Qwen2.5-VL and LLaVA-v1.5 show that SIRA consistently reduces hallucinations while preserving descriptive coverage and incurring lower overhead than two-pass contrastive decoding. SIRA requires no training, external verifier, or perturbed input, and applies to open-weight LVLMs with white-box inference access.

AI.tldr: SIRA is a training-free internal contrastive decoding method that reduces hallucinations in LVLMs by masking late-layer image attention to create an internal language-prior reference, without requiring external perturbations or extra forward passes.
AI.motivation: LVLMs hallucinate when language priors dominate weak visual evidence; existing contrastive decoding uses externally perturbed images which introduce artifacts and require costly forward passes.
AI.method: SIRA forks a counterfactual branch in later transformer layers by masking image-token attention, retaining shared multimodal context but blocking late visual access, and uses token-level contrast between the full and branch paths during decoding.
AI.result: On POPE, CHAIR, and AMBER with Qwen2.5-VL and LLaVA-v1.5, SIRA consistently reduces hallucinations while preserving descriptive coverage and incurring lower overhead than two-pass contrastive decoding.
AI.conclusion: SIRA offers an effective, training-free, and low-overhead alternative to existing contrastive decoding for hallucination mitigation, applicable to white-box LVLMs without external resources.

title: UniTriGen: Unified Triplet Generation of Aligned Visible-Infrared-Label for Few-Shot RGB-T Semantic Segmentation
summary: RGB-T semantic segmentation requires strictly aligned VIS-IR-Label triplets; however, such aligned triplet data are often scarce in real-world scenarios. Existing generative augmentation methods usually adopt cascaded generation paradigms, decomposing joint triplet generation into local conditional processes. As a result, consistency among VIS, IR, and Label in spatial structure, semantic content, and cross-modal details cannot be reliably maintained. To address this issue, we propose UniTriGen, a unified triplet generation framework that directly generates spatially aligned, semantically consistent, and modality complementary VIS-IR-Label triplets under the guidance of text prompts. UniTriGen first introduces a unified triplet generation mechanism, where VIS, IR, and Label are jointly encoded into a shared latent space and modeled with a diffusion process to enforce global cross-modal consistency. Lightweight modality-specific residual adapters are further integrated into this mechanism to accommodate modality-specific imaging characteristics and output formats. To mitigate generation bias caused by imbalanced scene and class distributions in limited paired triplets, UniTriGen also employs a scene-balanced and class-aware few-shot sampling strategy, which induces a more balanced sampling distribution and enhances the scene and class diversity of generated triplets. Experiments show that UniTriGen generates high-quality aligned triplets from limited real paired data, thereby achieving consistent performance improvements across various RGB-T semantic segmentation models.

AI.tldr: UniTriGen is a unified framework that generates aligned VIS-IR-Label triplets from limited data using diffusion models and balanced sampling, improving RGB-T semantic segmentation performance.
AI.motivation: RGB-T semantic segmentation requires scarce aligned VIS-IR-Label triplets; existing cascaded generation methods fail to maintain consistency among the three modalities.
AI.method: Proposes UniTriGen with (1) a unified triplet generation mechanism encoding VIS, IR, Label into shared latent space with diffusion, (2) lightweight modality-specific residual adapters, and (3) a scene-balanced class-aware few-shot sampling strategy.
AI.result: Generates high-quality aligned triplets from limited real paired data, achieving consistent performance improvements across various RGB-T semantic segmentation models.
AI.conclusion: UniTriGen effectively addresses the data scarcity problem in RGB-T segmentation by generating consistent triplets, outperforming cascaded approaches.

title: MultiEmo-Bench: Multi-label Visual Emotion Analysis for Multi-modal Large Language Models
summary: This paper introduces a multi-label visual emotion analysis benchmark dataset for comprehensively evaluating the ability of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to predict the emotions evoked by images. Recent user studies report an unintuitive finding: humans may prefer the predictions of MLLMs over the labels in existing datasets. We argue that this phenomenon stems from the suboptimal annotation scheme used in existing datasets, where each annotator is shown a single candidate emotion for each image and judges whether it is evoked or not. This approach is clearly limited because a single image can evoke multiple emotions with varying intensities. As a result, evaluations based on these datasets may underestimate the capabilities of MLLMs, yet an appropriate benchmark for evaluating such models remains lacking. To address this issue, we introduce a new multi-label benchmark dataset for visual emotion analysis toward MLLMs evaluation. We hire $20$ annotators per image and ask them to select all emotions they feel from an image. Then, we aggregate the votes across all annotators, providing a more reliable and representative dataset labeled with a distribution of emotions. The resulting dataset contains $10,344$ images with $236,998$ valid votes across eight emotions. Based on this benchmark dataset, we evaluate several recent models, including Qwen3-VL, OpenAI's GPT, Gemini, and Claude. We assess model performance on both dominant emotion prediction and emotion distribution prediction. Our results demonstrate the progress achieved by recent MLLMs while also indicating that substantial room for improvement remains. Furthermore, our experiments with LLM-as-a-judge show that the method does not consistently improve MLLMs' performance, indicating its limitations for the subjective task of visual emotion analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a multi-label benchmark dataset for visual emotion analysis, addressing limitations in existing datasets where annotators judged single candidate emotions per image. The new dataset uses 20 annotators per image who select all evoked emotions, yielding 10,344 images with 236,998 votes across eight emotions. Evaluations of recent MLLMs show progress but substantial room for improvement.
AI.motivation: Existing visual emotion datasets use suboptimal annotation schemes (single candidate emotion per annotator), leading to evaluations that underestimate MLLM capabilities. User studies unexpectedly show humans prefer MLLM predictions over existing dataset labels, motivating the need for a better benchmark.
AI.method: (1) Collected 10,344 images with 20 annotators each. (2) Annotators selected all emotions felt (multi-label, not single-candidate). (3) Aggregated votes across annotators to produce emotion distributions. (4) Evaluated recent MLLMs (Qwen3-VL, GPT, Gemini, Claude) on dominant emotion prediction and emotion distribution prediction tasks.
AI.result: Recent MLLMs show progress in visual emotion analysis but substantial room for improvement remains. LLM-as-a-judge method does not consistently improve performance on this subjective task.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-label benchmark provides a more reliable evaluation for MLLMs in visual emotion analysis. Current models still have significant limitations, and LLM-as-a-judge has limited utility for this subjective task.

title: How to Evaluate and Refine your CAM
summary:
Class attribution maps (CAMs) provide local explanations for the decisions of convolutional neural networks. While widely used in practice, the evaluation of CAMs remains challenging due to the lack of ground-truth explanations, making it difficult to evaluate the soundness of existing metrics. Independently, most commonly used CAM methods produce low-resolution attribution maps, which limits their usefulness for detailed interpretability.
  To address the evaluation challenge, we introduce a synthetic dataset with ground-truth attributions that enables a rigorous comparison of CAM evaluation metrics. Using this dataset, we analyze existing metrics and propose ARCC, a new composite metric that more reliably identifies faithful explanations. To address the low resolution issue, we introduce RefineCAM, a method that produces high-resolution attribution maps by aggregating CAMs across multiple network layers. Our results show that RefineCAM consistently outperforms existing methods according to the proposed evaluation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a synthetic dataset for evaluating CAM metrics, proposes ARCC metric for faithful explanations, and presents RefineCAM for high-resolution attribution maps.
AI.motivation: CAM evaluation lacks ground-truth explanations, making existing metrics unreliable; CAM methods also produce low-resolution maps.
AI.method: Synthetic dataset with ground-truth attributions; ARCC composite metric; RefineCAM aggregates CAMs across layers for high-resolution maps.
AI.result: ARCC more reliably identifies faithful explanations; RefineCAM outperforms existing methods per the proposed evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Proposed evaluation framework and high-resolution CAM method advance reliable interpretability in CNNs.

title: Vision-Based Water Level and Flow Estimation
summary: With the rapid evolution of computer vision, vision-based methodologies for water level and river surface velocity estimation have reached significant maturity. Compared to traditional sensing, these techniques offer superior interpretability, automated data archiving, and enhanced system robustness. However, challenges such as environmental sensitivity, limited precision, and complex site calibration persist. This work proposes an integrated framework that synergizes state-of-the-art (SOTA) vision models with statistical modeling. By leveraging physical priors and robust filtering strategies, we improve the accuracy of water level detection and flow estimation. Code will be available at https://github.com/sunzx97/Vision_Based_Water_Level_and_Flow_Estimation.git

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an integrated framework combining SOTA vision models with statistical modeling to improve water level detection and flow estimation, addressing challenges like environmental sensitivity and limited precision.
AI.motivation: Vision-based methods for water level and velocity estimation have matured but still face challenges such as environmental sensitivity, limited precision, and complex site calibration.
AI.method: An integrated framework that synergizes SOTA vision models with statistical modeling, leveraging physical priors and robust filtering strategies.
AI.result: Improved accuracy of water level detection and flow estimation compared to traditional vision-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively enhances robustness and precision of vision-based water monitoring, with code publicly available.

title: TERRA-CD: Multi-Temporal Framework for Multi-class and Semantic Change Detection
comment: Paper presented at 11th International Congress on Information and Communication Technology (ICICT) 2026, London
summary: Urban vegetation monitoring plays a vital role in understanding environmental changes, yet comprehensive datasets for this purpose remain limited. To address this gap, we present the Temporal Remote-sensing Repository for Analyzing Change Detection (TERRA-CD), a benchmark dataset comprising 5,221 Sentinel-2 image pairs from 2019 and 2024, covering 232 cities across the USA and Europe. The dataset features three distinct annotation schemes: 4-class land cover mapping masks, 3-class vegetation change masks, and 13-class semantic change masks capturing all possible land cover transitions. Using various deep learning approaches including Siamese networks, STANet variants, Bi-SRNet, Changemask, Post-Classification Comparison, and HRSCD strategies, we evaluated the dataset's effectiveness for both vegetation Multi-class Change Detection as well as Semantic Change Detection. The proposed dataset and methods are available at https://github.com/omkarsoak/TERRA-CD.

AI.tldr: TERRA-CD is a benchmark dataset of 5,221 Sentinel-2 image pairs (2019-2024) across 232 US/European cities, with three annotation schemes (4-class land cover, 3-class vegetation change, 13-class semantic change masks), evaluated using multiple deep learning approaches for change detection.
AI.motivation: Urban vegetation monitoring is crucial for understanding environmental changes, but comprehensive datasets for this purpose remain limited, motivating the creation of a large-scale benchmark dataset.
AI.method: Constructed from 5,221 Sentinel-2 image pairs from 2019 and 2024 covering 232 cities; features three annotation schemes (4-class land cover, 3-class vegetation change, 13-class semantic change masks); evaluated using Siamese networks, STANet variants, Bi-SRNet, Changemask, Post-Classification Comparison, and HRSCD strategies.
AI.result: The dataset was evaluated on both vegetation Multi-class Change Detection and Semantic Change Detection tasks using various deep learning approaches, demonstrating its effectiveness as a benchmark.
AI.conclusion: TERRA-CD provides a valuable benchmark for urban vegetation change detection, with the dataset and methods made publicly available at the provided GitHub repository.

title: Beyond Instance-Level Self-Supervision in 3D Multi-Modal Medical Imaging
summary: Self-supervised pre-training methods in medical imaging typically treat each individual as an isolated instance, learning representations through augmentation-based objectives or masked reconstruction. They often do not adequately capitalize on a key characteristic of physiological features: anatomical structures maintain consistent spatial relationships across individuals (instances), such as the thalamus being medial to the basal ganglia, regardless of variations in brain size, shape, or pathology. We propose leveraging this cross-instance topological consistency as a supervisory signal. The challenge arises from the inherent variability in medical imaging, which can differ significantly across instances and modalities. To tackle this, we focus on two alignment regimes. (i) Intra-instance: with pixel-level correspondences available, a cross-modal triplet objective explicitly preserves local neighborhood topology. (ii) Inter-instance: without such supervision, we derive pseudo-correspondences to control partial neighborhood alignment and prevent topology collapse across modalities. We validate our approach across 7 downstream multi-modal tasks, achieving average improvements of 1.1% and 5.94% in segmentation and classification tasks, respectively, and demonstrating significantly better robustness when modalities are missing at test time.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised pre-training method leveraging cross-instance topological consistency of anatomical structures across individuals, using intra-instance cross-modal triplet objectives and inter-instance pseudo-correspondences, achieving improvements of 1.1% in segmentation and 5.94% in classification across 7 multi-modal tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing self-supervised methods treat individuals as isolated instances, failing to exploit the consistent spatial relationships of anatomical structures across different individuals (e.g., thalamus always medial to basal ganglia). The authors aim to leverage this cross-instance topological consistency as a supervisory signal.
AI.method: Two alignment regimes: (i) Intra-instance: using pixel-level correspondences with a cross-modal triplet objective to preserve local neighborhood topology; (ii) Inter-instance: deriving pseudo-correspondences without explicit supervision to control partial neighborhood alignment and prevent topology collapse across modalities.
AI.result: Validated on 7 downstream multi-modal tasks, achieving average improvements of 1.1% in segmentation and 5.94% in classification, with significantly better robustness when modalities are missing at test time.
AI.conclusion: Cross-instance topological consistency is an effective supervisory signal for self-supervised pre-training in medical imaging, improving performance and robustness, particularly in scenarios with missing modalities.

title: MiVE: Multiscale Vision-language features for reference-guided video Editing
summary: Reference-guided video editing takes a source video, a text instruction, and a reference image as inputs, requiring the model to faithfully apply the instructed edits while preserving original motion and unedited content. Existing methods fall into two paradigms, each with inherent limitations: decoupled encoders suffer from modality gaps when processing instructions and visual content independently, while unified vision-language encoders lose fine-grained spatial details by relying solely on final-layer representations. We observe that VLM layers encode complementary information hierarchically -- early layers capture localized spatial details essential for precise editing, while deeper layers encode global semantics for instruction comprehension. Building on this insight, we present MiVE (Multiscale Vision-language features for reference-guided video Editing), a framework that repurposes VLMs as multiscale feature extractors. MiVE extracts hierarchical features from Qwen3-VL and integrates them into a unified self-attention Diffusion Transformer, eliminating the modality mismatch inherent in cross-attention designs. Experiments demonstrate that MiVE achieves state-of-the-art performance by ranking highest in human preference, outperforming both academic methods and commercial systems.

AI.tldr: MiVE is a reference-guided video editing framework that repurposes VLM (Qwen3-VL) as a multiscale feature extractor, hierarchically combining early-layer spatial details with deep-layer semantics in a unified self-attention Diffusion Transformer to achieve state-of-the-art performance over both academic and commercial systems.
AI.motivation: Existing reference-guided video editing methods suffer from either modality gaps (decoupled encoders) or loss of fine-grained spatial details (unified vision-language encoders using only final-layer representations). The authors observe that VLM layers encode hierarchical complementary information that can bridge this gap.
AI.method: MiVE repurposes VLMs (Qwen3-VL) as multiscale feature extractors, extracting hierarchical features from early layers (local spatial details) and deeper layers (global semantics), then integrating them into a unified self-attention Diffusion Transformer to avoid the modality mismatch in cross-attention designs.
AI.result: MiVE achieves state-of-the-art performance, ranking highest in human preference and outperforming both academic methods and commercial systems.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging multiscale hierarchical features from VLMs within a self-attention Diffusion Transformer effectively resolves modality mismatch and spatial detail loss, setting a new SOTA for reference-guided video editing.

title: Are Candidate Models Really Needed for Active Learning?
comment: Accepted for publication in Computer Vision and Image Understanding (CVIU)
summary: Deep learning has profoundly impacted domains such as computer vision and natural language processing by uncovering complex patterns in vast datasets. However, the reliance on extensive labeled data poses significant challenges, including resource constraints and annotation errors, particularly in training Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and transformers due to a larger number of parameters. Active learning offers a promising solution to reduce labeling burdens by strategically selecting the most informative samples for annotation. However, the current active learning frameworks are time-intensive which select the samples iteratively with the help of initial candidate models. This study investigates the feasibility of using CNNs and transformers with randomly initialized weights, eliminating the need for initial candidate models while achieving results comparable to active learning frameworks that depend on such candidate models. We evaluate three confidence-based sampling strategies: high confidence (HC), low confidence (LC), and a combination of high confidence in the early stages of training and low confidence at later stages of training (HCLC). Among these, mostly LC demonstrated the best performance in our experiments, showcasing its effectiveness as an active learning strategy without the need for candidate models. Further, extensive experiments verify the robustness of the proposed active learning methods. By challenging traditional frameworks, the proposed work introduces a streamlined approach to active learning, advancing efficiency and flexibility across diverse datasets and domains.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using randomly initialized CNNs and transformers with confidence-based sampling strategies (especially low confidence) for active learning, eliminating the need for initial candidate models while achieving comparable performance.
AI.motivation: Active learning reduces labeling burdens but traditional frameworks are time-intensive and rely on initial candidate models. This study aims to eliminate the need for such candidate models to improve efficiency.
AI.method: The authors use CNNs and transformers with randomly initialized weights, evaluating three confidence-based sampling strategies: high confidence (HC), low confidence (LC), and a hybrid HCLC approach.
AI.result: Low confidence (LC) sampling demonstrated the best performance, showing effectiveness as an active learning strategy without requiring candidate models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed streamlined active learning approach challenges traditional frameworks, advancing efficiency and flexibility across diverse datasets and domains.

title: EponaV2: Driving World Model with Comprehensive Future Reasoning
summary: Data scaling plays a pivotal role in the pursuit of general intelligence. However, the prevailing perception-planning paradigm in autonomous driving relies heavily on expensive manual annotations to supervise trajectory planning, which severely limits its scalability. Conversely, although existing perception-free driving world models achieve impressive driving performance, their real-world reasoning ability for planning is solely built on next frame image forecasting. Due to the lack of enough supervision, these models often struggle with comprehensive scene understanding, resulting in unsatisfactory trajectory planning. In this paper, we propose EponaV2, a novel paradigm of driving world models, which achieves high-quality planning with comprehensive future reasoning. Inspired by how human drivers anticipate 3D geometry and semantics, we train our model to forecast more comprehensive future representations, which can be additionally decoded to future geometry and semantic maps. Extracting the 3D and semantic modalities enables our model to deeply understand the surrounding environment, and the future prediction task significantly enhances the real-world reasoning capabilities of EponaV2, ultimately leading to improved trajectory planning. Moreover, inspired by the training recipe of Large Language Models (LLMs), we introduce a flow matching group relative policy optimization mechanism to further improve planning accuracy. The state-of-the-art (SOTA) performances of EponaV2 among perception-free models on three NAVSIM benchmarks (+1.3PDMS, +5.5EPDMS) demonstrate the effectiveness of our methods.

AI.tldr: EponaV2 is a perception-free driving world model that forecasts 3D geometry and semantics, combined with flow matching group relative policy optimization (inspired by LLM training), achieving SOTA planning performance on NAVSIM benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing perception-planning paradigms rely on expensive manual annotations for trajectory supervision, limiting scalability; meanwhile, perception-free world models only use next-frame image forecasting, lacking comprehensive scene understanding for reliable planning.
AI.method: EponaV2 forecasts future 3D geometry and semantic representations (rather than just pixels), decodes them into future geometry/semantic maps for deeper scene understanding, and applies flow matching group relative policy optimization inspired by LLM training to improve planning accuracy.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA performance among perception-free models on three NAVSIM benchmarks, with +1.3 PDMS and +5.5 EPDMS improvements.
AI.conclusion: Forecasting comprehensive future representations (3D geometry + semantics) combined with LLM-inspired policy optimization significantly enhances planning capabilities in driving world models without requiring expensive perception annotations.

title: Generating HDR Video from SDR Video
summary: The high dynamic range (HDR) video ecosystem is approaching maturity, but the problem of upconverting legacy standard dynamic range (SDR) videos persists without a convincing solution. We propose a framework for HDR video synthesis from casual SDR footage by leveraging large-scale generative video models. We introduce a Multi-Exposure Video Model (MEVM) that can predict exposure-bracketed linear SDR video sequences from a single nonlinear SDR video input. We further propose a learnable Video Merging Model (VMM) that merges the predicted exposure-bracketed video into a high-quality HDR sequence while preserving detail in both shadows and highlights. Extensive experiments, quantitative and qualitative evaluation, and a user study demonstrate that our approach enables robust HDR conversion for in-the-wild examples from casual consumer videos and even iconic films. Finally, our model can support HDR synthesis pipelines built upon existing SDR generative video models. Output HDR videos can be viewed on our supplementary webpage: sdr2hdrvideo.github.io

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework to convert standard dynamic range (SDR) videos to high dynamic range (HDR) videos using a Multi-Exposure Video Model (MEVM) that predicts exposure-bracketed sequences from a single SDR input, combined with a learnable Video Merging Model (VMM) to produce high-quality HDR output, enabling robust conversion for casual and professional videos.
AI.motivation: The HDR video ecosystem is maturing, but there is no convincing solution for upconverting legacy SDR videos to HDR format, creating a gap between existing SDR content and modern HDR displays.
AI.method: The framework consists of two components: (1) a Multi-Exposure Video Model (MEVM) that predicts exposure-bracketed linear SDR video sequences from a single nonlinear SDR input, leveraging large-scale generative video models; and (2) a learnable Video Merging Model (VMM) that merges the predicted exposures into a high-quality HDR video while preserving details in shadows and highlights.
AI.result: Extensive experiments, quantitative and qualitative evaluations, and a user study demonstrate robust HDR conversion for in-the-wild examples from casual consumer videos and iconic films. The model also supports HDR synthesis pipelines built upon existing SDR generative video models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides a convincing solution for SDR-to-HDR video conversion, leveraging generative video models to handle diverse real-world content, with demonstrated results on both casual and professional videos.

title: SceneFunRI: Reasoning the Invisible for Task-Driven Functional Object Localization
summary: In real-world scenes, target objects may reside in regions that are not visible. While humans can often infer the locations of occluded objects from context and commonsense knowledge, this capability remains a major challenge for vision-language models (VLMs). To address this gap, we introduce SceneFunRI, a benchmark for Reasoning the Invisible. Based on the SceneFun3D dataset, SceneFunRI formulates the task as a 2D spatial reasoning problem via a semi-automatic pipeline and comprises 855 instances. It requires models to infer the locations of invisible functional objects from task instructions and commonsense reasoning. The strongest baseline model (Gemini 3 Flash) only achieves an CAcc@75 of 15.20, an mIoU of 0.74, and a Dist of 28.65. We group our prompting analysis into three categories: Strong Instruction Prompting, Reasoning-based Prompting, and Spatial Process of Elimination (SPoE). These findings indicate that invisible-region reasoning remains an unstable capability in current VLMs, motivating future work on models that more tightly integrate task intent, commonsense priors, spatial grounding, and uncertainty-aware search.

AI.tldr: SceneFunRI is a new benchmark for evaluating VLMs on reasoning about occluded/invisible objects using commonsense and context, showing that current models (e.g., Gemini 3 Flash) perform poorly, with the best CAcc@75 only 15.20.
AI.motivation: Humans can infer occluded object locations from context, but VLMs struggle with this. The paper aims to bridge this gap by creating a benchmark for reasoning about invisible functional objects.
AI.method: Built on SceneFun3D, the benchmark uses a semi-automatic pipeline to create 855 2D spatial reasoning instances requiring models to infer locations of invisible objects from task instructions and commonsense.
AI.result: Best model (Gemini 3 Flash) achieves CAcc@75 of 15.20, mIoU of 0.74, and Dist of 28.65. Prompting analysis categories: Strong Instruction, Reasoning-based, and Spatial Process of Elimination (SPoE).
AI.conclusion: Invisible-region reasoning remains an unstable capability in current VLMs, motivating future work integrating task intent, commonsense priors, spatial grounding, and uncertainty-aware search.

title: Towards Continuous Sign Language Conversation from Isolated Signs
summary: Sign language is the primary language for many Deaf and Hard-of-Hearing (DHH) signers, yet most conversational AI systems still mediate interaction through spoken or written language. This spoken-language-centered interface can limit access for signers for whom spoken or written language is not the most accessible medium, motivating direct sign-to-sign conversational modeling. However, sentence-level sign video data are expensive to collect and annotate, leaving existing sign translation and production models with limited vocabulary coverage and weak open-domain generalization. We address this bottleneck by constructing continuous sign conversations from isolated signs: large-scale labeled isolated clips are collected as lexically grounded motion primitives and recomposed into sign-language-ordered utterances derived from existing dialogue corpora. We introduce SignaVox-W, which provides, to our knowledge, the largest labeled isolated-sign vocabulary to date, and SignaVox-U, a continuous 3D sign conversation dataset built from SignaVox-W. To bridge structural mismatch between spoken and signed languages, we use a retrieval-guided spoken-to-gloss translator; to bridge independently collected isolated clips, we propose BRAID, a diffusion Transformer that performs duration alignment and co-articulatory boundary inpainting. With the resulting data, we train SignaVox, a direct sign-to-sign conversational model that generates 3D body, hand, and facial motion responses from prior signing context without spoken-language text or externally provided glosses at inference time. Quantitative and qualitative evaluations show improved isolated-to-continuous motion quality, stronger response-level semantic alignment, and scalable signer-centered interaction that better supports visual-spatial articulation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SignaVox, a direct sign-to-sign conversational AI system that bypasses spoken language by constructing continuous sign conversations from large-scale isolated sign video data, using a retrieval-guided gloss translator and a diffusion Transformer (BRAID) for duration alignment and co-articulatory inpainting.
AI.motivation: Current conversational AI relies on spoken/written language, limiting accessibility for Deaf and Hard-of-Hearing signers. Sentence-level sign video data is expensive and scarce, resulting in limited vocabulary coverage and weak open-domain generalization for sign translation/production models.
AI.method: (1) SignaVox-W: large-scale labeled isolated-sign vocabulary dataset; (2) SignaVox-U: continuous 3D sign conversation dataset built by recomposing isolated clips into sign-language-ordered utterances from dialogue corpora; (3) Retrieval-guided spoken-to-gloss translator to bridge spoken-signed structural mismatch; (4) BRAID: diffusion Transformer for duration alignment and co-articulatory boundary inpainting.
AI.result: Improved isolated-to-continuous motion quality, stronger response-level semantic alignment, and scalable signer-centered interaction supporting visual-spatial articulation without requiring spoken-language text or externally provided glosses at inference time.
AI.conclusion: Direct sign-to-sign conversational modeling is a viable and scalable approach for building accessible AI systems for DHH signers, overcoming data scarcity through isolated-sign recomposition with appropriate motion synthesis techniques.

title: StyleTextGen: Style-Conditioned Multilingual Scene Text Generation
comment: This paper has been accepted to CVPR 2026
summary: Style-conditioned scene text generation faces unique challenges in extracting precise text styles from complex backgrounds and maintaining fine-grained style consistency across characters, especially for multilingual scripts. We propose StyleTextGen, a novel framework that learns to perceive and replicate visual text styles across different languages and writing systems. Our approach features three key contributions: First, we introduce a dual-branch style encoder dedicated to style modeling, yielding robust multilingual text style representations in complex real-world scenes. Second, we design a text style consistency loss that enhances style coherence and improves overall visual quality. Third, we develop a mask-guided inference strategy that ensures precise style alignment between generated and reference text. To facilitate systematic evaluation, we construct StyleText-CE, a bilingual scene text style benchmark covering both monolingual and cross-lingual settings. Extensive experiments demonstrate that StyleTextGen significantly outperforms existing methods in style consistency and cross-lingual generalization, establishing new state-of-the-art performance in multilingual style-conditioned text generation.

AI.tldr: StyleTextGen is a novel multilingual scene text generation framework with a dual-branch style encoder, text style consistency loss, and mask-guided inference, outperforming existing methods on the newly constructed StyleText-CE benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing scene text generation methods struggle with extracting precise text styles from complex backgrounds and maintaining fine-grained style consistency across characters, especially for multilingual scripts.
AI.method: (1) Dual-branch style encoder for robust multilingual style representation; (2) Text style consistency loss for enhanced style coherence; (3) Mask-guided inference strategy for precise style alignment; plus a new benchmark StyleText-CE.
AI.result: StyleTextGen significantly outperforms existing methods in style consistency and cross-lingual generalization, achieving new state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses multilingual style-conditioned text generation challenges and sets a new performance standard.

title: Breaking Dual Bottlenecks: Evolving Unified Multimodal Models into Self-Adaptive Interleaved Visual Reasoners
summary: Recent unified models integrate multimodal understanding and generation within a single framework. However, an "understanding-generation gap" persists, where models can capture user intent but often fail to translate this semantic knowledge into precise pixel-level manipulation. This gap results in two bottlenecks in anything-to-image task (X2I): the attention entanglement bottleneck, where blind planning struggles with complex prompts, and the visual refinement bottleneck, where unstructured feedback fails to correct imperfections efficiently. In this paper, we propose a novel framework that empowers unified models to autonomously switch between generation strategies based on instruction complexity and model capability. To achieve this, we construct a hierarchical data pipeline that constructs execution paths across three adaptive modes: direct generation for simple cases, self-reflection for quality refinement, and multi-step planning for decomposing complex scenarios. Building on this pipeline, we contribute a high-quality dataset with over 50,000 samples and implement a two-stage training strategy comprising SFT and RL. Specifically, we design step-wise reasoning rewards to ensure logical consistency and intra-group complexity penalty to prevent redundant computational overhead. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms existing baselines on X2I, achieving superior generation fidelity among simple-to-complex instructions. The code is released at https://github.com/WeChatCV/Interleaved_Visual_Reasoner.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework that enables unified multimodal models to autonomously switch between three generation strategies (direct generation, self-reflection, multi-step planning) based on task complexity, using a hierarchical data pipeline and two-stage training (SFT+RL) with step-wise reasoning rewards, achieving superior performance on anything-to-image tasks.
AI.motivation: The "understanding-generation gap" in unified multimodal models means they understand user intent but fail at precise pixel-level manipulation, leading to attention entanglement (struggling with complex prompts) and visual refinement bottlenecks (inefficient correction of imperfections).
AI.method: 1) A hierarchical data pipeline constructing execution paths across three adaptive modes: direct generation (simple), self-reflection (quality refinement), and multi-step planning (complex decomposition). 2) A high-quality dataset (50K+ samples). 3) Two-stage training: SFT followed by RL with step-wise reasoning rewards (logical consistency) and intra-group complexity penalty (prevent redundancy).
AI.result: Outperforms existing baselines on anything-to-image tasks across simple-to-complex instructions, achieving superior generation fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive framework effectively bridges the understanding-generation gap by enabling models to dynamically choose generation strategies, improving both fidelity and efficiency in X2I tasks.

title: Vision-Core Guided Contrastive Learning for Balanced Multi-modal Prognosis Prediction of Stroke
summary: Deep learning and multi-modal fusion have demonstrated transformative potential in medical diagnosis by integrating diverse data sources. However, accurate prognosis for ischemic stroke remains challenging due to limitations in existing multi-modal approaches. First, current methods are predominantly confined to dual-modal fusion, lacking a framework that effectively integrates the trifecta of medical images, structured clinical data, and unstructured text. Second, they often fail to establish deep bidirectional interactions between modalities; To address these critical gaps, this paper proposes a novel tri-modal fusion model for ischemic stroke prognosis. Our approach first enriches the data representation by employing a Large Language Model (LLM) to automatically generate semi-structured diagnostic text from brain MRIs. This process not only addresses the scarcity of expert annotations but also serves as a regularized semantic enhancement, improving multimodal fusion robustness. Furthermore, we design a core component termed the Vision-Conditioned Dual Alignment Fusion Module (VDAFM), which strategically uses visual features as a conditional prior to guide fine-grained interaction with the generated text. This module achieves a dynamic and profound fusion through a dual semantic alignment loss, effectively mitigating modal heterogeneity. Extensive experiments on a real-world clinical dataset demonstrate that our model achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a tri-modal fusion model for ischemic stroke prognosis that integrates medical images, clinical data, and LLM-generated text, featuring a Vision-Conditioned Dual Alignment Fusion Module (VDAFM) for deep bidirectional interactions, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Current multi-modal approaches for ischemic stroke prognosis are limited to dual-modal fusion and fail to establish deep bidirectional interactions between modalities, leaving a gap in effectively integrating the trifecta of medical images, structured clinical data, and unstructured text.
AI.method: The method uses an LLM to generate semi-structured diagnostic text from brain MRIs for data enrichment and regularization, and introduces a Vision-Conditioned Dual Alignment Fusion Module (VDAFM) that uses visual features as a conditional prior to guide fine-grained interaction with generated text via dual semantic alignment loss.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on a real-world clinical dataset demonstrate that the model achieves state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed tri-modal fusion framework effectively addresses limitations of prior dual-modal approaches by enabling deep bidirectional interactions, leveraging LLM-based text generation to enhance data representation, and achieving superior prognosis accuracy.

title: Towards Label-Free Single-Cell Phenotyping Using Multi-Task Learning
comment: Accepted in 28th International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR) 2026
summary: Label-free single-cell imaging offers a scalable, non-invasive alternative to fluorescence-based cytometry, yet inferring molecular phenotypes directly from bright-field morphology remains challenging. We present a unified Deep Learning (DL) framework that jointly performs White Blood Cell (WBC) classification and continuous protein-expression regression from label-free Differential Phase Contrast (DPC) images. Our model employs a Hybrid architecture that fuses convolutional fine-grained texture features with transformer-based global representations through a learnable cross-branch gating module, enabling robust morpho-molecular inference from DPC images. To support downstream interpretability, we further incorporate a Large Language Model (LLM) that generates concise, biologically grounded summaries of the predicted cell states. Experiments on the Berkeley Single Cell Computational Microscopy (BSCCM) and Blood Cells Image benchmarks demonstrate strong performance, achieving a 91.3% WBC classification accuracy and a 0.72 Pearson correlation for CD16 expression regression on BSCCM. These results underscore the promise of label-free single-cell imaging for cost-effective hematological profiling, enabling simultaneous phenotype identification and quantitative biomarker estimation without fluorescent staining. The source code is available at https://github.com/saqibnaziir/Single-Cell-Phenotyping.

AI.tldr: A deep learning framework jointly performs WBC classification and protein expression regression from label-free DPC images using a hybrid CNN-transformer architecture with an LLM for interpretable summaries, achieving 91.3% accuracy and 0.72 Pearson correlation.
AI.motivation: To enable cost-effective, non-invasive hematological profiling by inferring molecular phenotypes (both cell type and protein expression) from label-free single-cell images, eliminating the need for fluorescent staining.
AI.method: A hybrid DL architecture combining convolutional neural networks for fine-grained texture features with transformers for global representations, connected via a learnable cross-branch gating module. An LLM generates biologically grounded summaries of predicted cell states.
AI.result: 91.3% WBC classification accuracy and 0.72 Pearson correlation for CD16 expression regression on the BSCCM benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Label-free single-cell imaging combined with the proposed DL framework is a promising approach for simultaneous phenotype identification and quantitative biomarker estimation without fluorescent staining.

title: CHASM: Cross-frequency Harmonized Axis-Separable Mixing for Spectral Token Operators
summary: Spectral token mixers based on Fourier transforms provide an efficient way to model global interactions in visual feature maps. Existing designs often either apply filter-wise spectral responses along fixed channel axes, or learn adaptive frequency-indexed channel mixing without explicitly aligning the channel directions used across frequencies. We propose CHASM, a Cross-frequency Harmonized Axis-Separable Mixer, as a structured middle ground. CHASM separates what should be shared from what should remain frequency-specific: all frequencies share a learned channel eigenbasis, while each frequency retains its own positive spectral gains. The shared basis makes channel directions comparable across the spectrum, whereas the positive gains preserve local spectral adaptivity. CHASM applies this structured operator separably along the height and width axes and is used as a drop-in replacement mixer inside existing backbones. We provide a structural characterization of the shared-basis operator family and evaluate CHASM through controlled same-backbone comparisons. Across accelerated MRI reconstruction, undersampled MRI segmentation, and natural-image reconstruction, CHASM consistently improves over same-backbone spectral-mixer baselines. Ablations show that removing the shared-basis constraint weakens performance, and randomizing coherent sampling geometry substantially reduces the gain, supporting cross-frequency harmonization as a useful inductive bias for spectral token operators.

AI.tldr: CHASM introduces a cross-frequency harmonized spectral token mixer that separates a shared channel eigenbasis (common across frequencies) from frequency-specific positive gains, applied separably along spatial axes, improving performance in MRI reconstruction, segmentation, and natural-image reconstruction over existing spectral mixers.
AI.motivation: Existing spectral token mixers either apply filter-wise spectral responses along fixed channel axes or learn adaptive frequency-indexed channel mixing without aligning channel directions across frequencies, lacking a structured way to share information across frequencies.
AI.method: CHASM separates a learned shared channel eigenbasis (common across all frequencies) from frequency-specific positive spectral gains, applied separably along height and width axes as a drop-in replacement mixer in existing backbones.
AI.result: CHASM consistently outperforms same-backbone spectral-mixer baselines across accelerated MRI reconstruction, undersampled MRI segmentation, and natural-image reconstruction. Ablations show removing shared-basis weakens performance, and randomizing sampling geometry reduces gain.
AI.conclusion: Cross-frequency harmonization through a shared channel eigenbasis is a useful inductive bias for spectral token operators, providing a structured middle ground between fixed-channel and fully adaptive frequency mixing.

title: Video-Zero: Self-Evolution Video Understanding
summary: Self-evolution offers a promising path for improving reasoning models without relying on intensive human annotation. However, extending this paradigm to video understanding remains underexplored and challenging: videos are long, dynamic, and redundant, while the evidence needed for reasoning is often sparse and temporally localized. Naively generating difficult question-answer pairs from full videos can therefore produce supervision that appears challenging but is weakly grounded, relying on static cues or language priors rather than temporal evidence. In this work, we argue that the key bottleneck of video self-evolution is not difficulty alone, but grounding. We propose Video-Zero, an annotation-free Questioner--Solver co-evolution framework that centers self-evolution on temporally localized evidence. The Questioner discovers informative evidence segments and generates evidence-grounded questions, while the Solver learns to answer and align its predictions with the supporting evidence. This closes an iterative loop of evidence discovery, grounded supervision, and evidence-aligned learning. Across 13 benchmarks spanning temporal grounding, long-video understanding, and video reasoning, Video-Zero consistently improves multiple video VLM backbones, demonstrating the effectiveness and transferability of evidence-centered self-evolution.

AI.tldr: Video-Zero is an annotation-free self-evolution framework for video understanding that uses a Questioner-Solver co-evolution loop centered on temporally localized evidence, improving reasoning models across 13 benchmarks without human annotation.
AI.motivation: Extending self-evolution (a promising paradigm for improving reasoning models without human annotation) to video understanding is challenging because videos are long, dynamic, and redundant. Naively generating difficult QA pairs from full videos produces weakly grounded supervision that relies on static cues or language priors rather than temporal evidence.
AI.method: Video-Zero proposes a Questioner-Solver co-evolution framework where: (1) The Questioner discovers informative evidence segments and generates evidence-grounded questions, (2) The Solver learns to answer and align its predictions with supporting evidence, closing an iterative loop of evidence discovery, grounded supervision, and evidence-aligned learning.
AI.result: Across 13 benchmarks spanning temporal grounding, long-video understanding, and video reasoning, Video-Zero consistently improves multiple video VLM backbones, demonstrating effectiveness and transferability.
AI.conclusion: The key bottleneck of video self-evolution is not difficulty alone, but grounding. Evidence-centered self-evolution is effective and transferable for improving video reasoning models.

title: EARL: Towards a Unified Analysis-Guided Reinforcement Learning Framework for Egocentric Interaction Reasoning and Pixel Grounding
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026. Project page: https://github.com/yuggiehk/EARL
summary: Understanding human--environment interactions from egocentric vision is essential for assistive robotics and embodied intelligent agents, yet existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) still struggle with accurate interaction reasoning and fine-grained pixel grounding. To this end, this paper introduces EARL, an Egocentric Analysis-guided Reinforcement Learning framework that explicitly transfers coarse interaction semantics to query-oriented answering and grounding. Specifically, EARL adopts a two-stage parsing framework including coarse-grained interpretation and fine-grained response. The first stage holistically interprets egocentric interactions and generates a structured textual description. The second stage produces the textual answer and pixel-level mask in response to the user query. To bridge the two stages, we extract a global interaction descriptor as a semantic prior, which is integrated via a novel Analysis-guided Feature Synthesizer (AFS) for query-oriented reasoning. To optimize heterogeneous outputs, including textual answers, bounding boxes, and grounding masks, we design a multi-faceted reward function and train the response stage with GRPO. Experiments on Ego-IRGBench show that EARL achieves 65.48% cIoU for pixel grounding, outperforming previous RL-based methods by 8.37%, while OOD grounding results on EgoHOS indicate strong transferability to unseen egocentric grounding scenarios.

AI.tldr: EARL is a two-stage reinforcement learning framework that improves egocentric interaction understanding by first generating coarse semantic descriptions, then using an Analysis-guided Feature Synthesizer and GRPO-based multi-reward training to produce accurate textual answers and pixel-level masks, achieving 65.48% cIoU on Ego-IRGBench (+8.37% over prior RL methods) with strong OOD transferability on EgoHOS.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) struggle with accurate interaction reasoning and fine-grained pixel grounding in egocentric vision, which is critical for assistive robotics and embodied AI.
AI.method: EARL uses a two-stage parsing framework: (1) coarse-grained interpretation generating structured textual descriptions of egocentric interactions, (2) fine-grained response producing textual answers and pixel-level masks. A global interaction descriptor bridges stages via Analysis-guided Feature Synthesizer (AFS). Training uses GRPO with a multi-faceted reward function for heterogeneous outputs (text, bounding boxes, masks).
AI.result: EARL achieves 65.48% cIoU for pixel grounding on Ego-IRGBench, outperforming previous RL-based methods by 8.37%. OOD grounding on EgoHOS demonstrates strong transferability to unseen egocentric scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The explicit transfer of coarse interaction semantics to query-oriented answering and grounding, combined with multi-reward GRPO training, significantly improves fine-grained egocentric interaction understanding and generalizes well to unseen scenarios.

title: BioHuman: Learning Biomechanical Human Representations from Video
summary: Understanding human motion beyond surface kinematics is crucial for motion analysis, rehabilitation, and injury risk assessment. However, progress in this domain is limited by the lack of large-scale datasets with biomechanical annotations, and by existing approaches that cannot directly infer internal biomechanical states from visual observations. In this paper, we introduce a simulation-based framework for estimating muscle activations from existing motion capture datasets, resulting in BioHuman10M, a large-scale dataset with synchronized video, motion, and activations. Building on BioHuman10M, we propose BioHuman, an end-to-end model that takes monocular video as input and jointly predicts human motion and muscle activations, effectively bridging visual observations and internal biomechanical states. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BioHuman enables accurate reconstruction of both kinematic motion and muscle activity, and generalizes across diverse subjects and motions. We believe our approach establishes a new benchmark for video-based biomechanical understanding and opens up new possibilities for physically grounded human modeling.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BioHuman, a simulation-based framework that creates a large-scale dataset (BioHuman10M) with synchronized video, motion, and muscle activation data, and proposes an end-to-end model that estimates both human motion and muscle activations directly from monocular video.
AI.motivation: Current motion analysis lacks large-scale datasets with biomechanical annotations, and existing methods cannot directly infer internal biomechanical states (e.g., muscle activations) from visual observations, limiting progress in rehabilitation and injury risk assessment.
AI.method: The authors develop a simulation-based framework to estimate muscle activations from existing motion capture datasets, producing BioHuman10M (a large-scale dataset with synchronized video, motion, and activations). They then propose BioHuman, an end-to-end model that takes monocular video as input and jointly predicts human motion and muscle activations.
