AI.motivation: Current scene perception methods produce isolated predictions (objects, parts, affordances) without capturing the structured dependencies needed for interaction-oriented scene understanding.
AI.method: SceneParser is a VLM-based parser trained for unified hierarchical generation using structural-completion pseudo labels and curriculum learning. A scalable data engine built SceneParser-Bench with 110K images and millions of hierarchical annotations.
AI.result: SceneParser achieves stronger structure-aware performance than existing MLLMs and perception-stitching pipelines on SceneParser-Bench, and remains compatible with conventional tasks like COCO and AGD20K.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical scene parsing provides an actionable representation for visual understanding, enabling interaction-oriented reasoning that isolated predictions cannot capture.

title: Road Maps as Free Geometric Priors: Weather-Invariant Drone Geo-Localization with GeoFuse
summary: Drone-view geo-localization aims to match a query drone image, often captured under adverse weather conditions (e.g., rain, snow, fog), against a gallery of geo-tagged satellite images. Weather-induced degradations in the drone view, such as noise, reduced visibility, and partial occlusions, severely exacerbate the intrinsic cross-view domain gap. While prior methods predominantly rely on weather-specific architectures or data augmentations, they have largely overlooked road map data, a readily available modality that provides strong, inherently weather-invariant geometric layout cues (e.g., road networks and building footprints) at negligible additional cost. We introduce GeoFuse, a cross-modal fusion framework that integrates precisely aligned road map tiles with satellite imagery to yield more discriminative and weather-resilient representations. We first augment the existing University-1652 and DenseUAV benchmarks with geo-aligned road maps, supplying structural priors robust to meteorological variations. Building on this, we propose a flexible fusion module that combines satellite and road map features via token-level and channel-level interactions, with a lightweight dynamic gating mechanism that adaptively weights modality contributions per instance. Finally, we employ class-level cross-view contrastive learning to promote robust alignment between weather-degraded drone features and the fused satellite-roadmap representations. Extensive experiments under diverse weather conditions show that GeoFuse consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving +3.46% and +23.18% Recall@1 accuracy on the University-1652 and DenseUAV benchmarks, respectively.

AI.tldr: GeoFuse is a cross-modal fusion framework that integrates road map tiles with satellite imagery to improve drone geo-localization under adverse weather, achieving SOTA results on two benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing drone geo-localization methods struggle under adverse weather due to domain gaps, and prior work overlooks readily available road map data that provides weather-invariant geometric cues.
AI.method: GeoFuse (1) augments benchmarks with geo-aligned road maps, (2) proposes a fusion module combining satellite and road map features via token-level and channel-level interactions with dynamic gating, and (3) employs class-level cross-view contrastive learning for robust alignment.
AI.result: Achieves +3.46% and +23.18% Recall@1 on University-1652 and DenseUAV benchmarks, outperforming SOTA methods under diverse weather conditions.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating road map data is a simple yet effective strategy for weather-resilient drone geo-localization, as demonstrated by GeoFuse's strong performance and flexibility.

title: SCRWKV: Ultra-Compact Structure-Calibrated Vision-RWKV for Topological Crack Segmentation
summary: Achieving pixel-level accurate segmentation of structural cracks across diverse scenarios remains a formidable challenge. Existing methods face significant bottlenecks in balancing crack topology modeling with computational efficiency, often failing to reconcile high segmentation quality with low resource demands. To address these limitations, we propose the Ultra-Compact Structure-Calibrated Vision RWKV (SCRWKV), a network that achieves high-precision modeling via a novel Structure-Field Encoder (SFE) backbone while maintaining linear complexity. The SFE integrates the Adaptive Multi-scale Cascaded Modulator (AMCM) to enhance texture representation and utilizes the Structure-Calibrated Insight Unit (SCIU) as its core engine. Specifically, the SCIU employs the Geometry-guided Bidirectional Structure Transformation (GBST) to capture topological correlations and integrates the Dynamic Self-Calibrating Decay (DSCD) into Dy-WKV to suppress noise propagation. Furthermore, we introduce a lightweight Cross-Scale Harmonic Fusion (CSHF) decoder to achieve precise feature aggregation. Systematic evaluations on multiple benchmarks characterized by complex textures and severe interference demonstrate that SCRWKV, with only 1.22M parameters, significantly outperforms SOTA methods. Achieving an F1 score of 0.8428 and mIoU of 0.8512 on the TUT dataset, the model confirms its robust potential for efficient real-world deployment. The code is available at https://github.com/zhxhzy/SCRWKV.

AI.tldr: SCRWKV is an ultra-compact network (1.22M params) for pixel-level crack segmentation that uses a Structure-Field Encoder backbone with Adaptive Multi-scale Cascaded Modulator and Structure-Calibrated Insight Unit to achieve high accuracy with linear complexity, outperforming SOTA on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing crack segmentation methods struggle to balance topology modeling with computational efficiency, failing to achieve both high segmentation quality and low resource demands simultaneously.
AI.method: Proposes SCRWKV with three key components: (1) Structure-Field Encoder (SFE) backbone using Adaptive Multi-scale Cascaded Modulator (AMCM) for texture enhancement and Structure-Calibrated Insight Unit (SCIU) as core engine; (2) SCIU employs Geometry-guided Bidirectional Structure Transformation (GBST) for topology and Dynamic Self-Calibrating Decay (DSCD) in Dy-WKV for noise suppression; (3) lightweight Cross-Scale Harmonic Fusion (CSHF) decoder for feature aggregation.
AI.result: SCRWKV achieves 0.8428 F1 and 0.8512 mIoU on TUT dataset, significantly outperforming SOTA methods with only 1.22M parameters, demonstrating robust performance across complex texture and interference scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SCRWKV effectively resolves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off in crack segmentation, showing strong potential for real-world deployment.

title: Multi-scale Coarse-to-fine Modeling for Test-time Human Motion Control
summary: We present MSCoT, a multi-scale, coarse-to-fine model for test-time human motion synthesis and control. Unlike recent approaches that rely on multiple iterative denoising/token-prediction steps, or modules tailored for specific control signals, MSCoT discretizes motion into a multi-scale hierarchical representation and predicts the entire token sequence at each temporal scale in a coarse-to-fine fashion. Building on this coarse-to-fine paradigm, we propose an efficient multi-scale token guidance strategy that overcomes the challenge of discrete sampling and steers the token distribution towards the control goals, allowing for fast and flexible control. To address the limitations of a discrete codebook, a lightweight token refiner further adds continuous residuals to the discrete token embeddings and allows differentiable test-time refinement optimization to ensure precise alignment with the control objectives. MSCoT is able to produce quality motions, consistent with the control constraints, while offering substantially faster sampling than diffusion-based approaches. Experiments on popular benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art controllable text-to-motion generation performance of MSCoT over existing baselines, with better motion quality (48% FID improvement), higher control accuracy (-61% avg error), and $10 \times$ faster inference speed on HumanML3D.

AI.tldr: MSCoT proposes a multi-scale coarse-to-fine model for controllable human motion synthesis that uses hierarchical token prediction, multi-scale token guidance for control, and a token refiner with continuous residuals, achieving state-of-the-art results with 48% FID improvement, 61% lower control error, and 10× faster inference than diffusion methods.
AI.motivation: Existing motion synthesis methods rely on iterative denoising/token-prediction or task-specific control modules, which are slow and lack flexibility. The goal is to develop a fast, unified framework for controllable motion generation with high quality and precise alignment to control constraints.
AI.method: 1) Multi-scale hierarchical representation that discretizes motion and predicts tokens from coarse to fine temporal scales. 2) Multi-scale token guidance strategy for efficient control during discrete sampling. 3) Lightweight token refiner that adds continuous residuals to discrete token embeddings for differentiable test-time refinement optimization.
AI.result: State-of-the-art controllable text-to-motion generation on benchmarks: 48% improvement in FID, 61% reduction in average control error, and 10× faster inference speed on HumanML3D compared to diffusion-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: MSCoT demonstrates that a coarse-to-fine discrete token prediction paradigm with multi-scale guidance and continuous residual refinement can outperform diffusion models in both quality and speed for controllable motion synthesis.

title: ACE-LoRA: Adaptive Orthogonal Decoupling for Continual Image Editing
summary: State-of-the-art diffusion models often rely on parameter-efficient fine-tuning to perform specialized image editing tasks. However, real-world applications require continual adaptation to new tasks while preserving previously learned knowledge. Despite the practical necessity, continual learning for image editing remains largely underexplored. We propose ACE-LoRA, a dynamic regularization framework for continual image editing that effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting. ACE-LoRA leverages Adaptive Orthogonal Decoupling to identify and orthogonalize task interference, and introduces a Rank-Invariant Historical Information Compression strategy to address scalability issues in continual updates. To facilitate continual learning in image editing and provide a standardized evaluation protocol, we introduce CIE-Bench, the first comprehensive benchmark in this domain. CIE-Bench encompasses diverse and practically relevant image editing scenarios with a balanced level of difficulty to effectively expose limitations of existing models while remaining compatible with parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms existing baselines in terms of instruction fidelity, visual realism, and robustness to forgetting, establishing a strong foundation for continual learning in image editing.

AI.tldr: ACE-LoRA is a dynamic regularization framework for continual image editing that prevents catastrophic forgetting via adaptive orthogonal decoupling and rank-invariant historical information compression, outperforming baselines on the new CIE-Bench benchmark.
AI.motivation: Real-world image editing requires continual adaptation to new tasks while preserving previously learned knowledge, yet continual learning for image editing remains largely underexplored and lacks standardized evaluation protocols.
AI.method: ACE-LoRA uses Adaptive Orthogonal Decoupling to identify and orthogonalize task interference, and Rank-Invariant Historical Information Compression to address scalability in continual updates. The authors also introduce CIE-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for continual image editing.
AI.result: ACE-LoRA consistently outperforms existing baselines in instruction fidelity, visual realism, and robustness to forgetting on the CIE-Bench benchmark.
AI.conclusion: ACE-LoRA establishes a strong foundation for continual learning in image editing by effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting in parameter-efficient fine-tuning scenarios.

title: A CUBS-Compatible Ultrasound Morphology and Uncertainty-Aware Baseline for Carotid Intima-Media Segmentation and Preliminary Risk Prediction
summary: Carotid atherosclerosis is a major contributor to ischemic stroke and transient ischemic attack. Conventional ultrasound assessment is commonly based on intima-media thickness, plaque appearance, stenosis degree, and peak systolic velocity, but these morphology- and velocity-based indicators may not fully capture patient-specific vascular risk. This study presents AtheroFlow-XNet, a CUBS-compatible ultrasound morphology and uncertainty-aware learning baseline for carotid intima-media segmentation and preliminary risk prediction. Using the Carotid Ultrasound Boundary Study dataset, manual lumen-intima and media-adventitia boundary annotations were converted into dense intima-media masks for supervised segmentation. Clinical variables were incorporated into an auxiliary risk-prediction branch, and Monte Carlo dropout was used for uncertainty-aware inference. The model was evaluated using a patient-level train-validation-test split with 1,522 training images, 326 validation images, and 328 testing images. The proposed model achieved a Dice coefficient of 0.7930 for LI-MA mask segmentation, a segmentation loss of 0.2359, and an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.6910 for preliminary risk prediction. Qualitative results showed that predicted masks were generally aligned with manual annotations, while uncertainty maps highlighted ambiguous wall-boundary regions. These results suggest that ultrasound-derived carotid morphology can support automated wall analysis and uncertainty-aware interpretation. Since CUBS does not provide Doppler waveforms or CFD-derived hemodynamic biomarkers, this work should be interpreted as a reproducible morphology-driven baseline. Future work will incorporate Doppler-derived flow profiles, patient-specific vascular reconstruction, and CFD-based wall shear biomarkers.

AI.tldr: AtheroFlow-XNet is a CUBS-compatible deep learning model for carotid intima-media segmentation and risk prediction using ultrasound, achieving 0.793 Dice and 0.691 AUC, with uncertainty quantification via Monte Carlo dropout.
AI.motivation: Conventional ultrasound assessment based on intima-media thickness, plaque appearance, stenosis degree, and peak systolic velocity may not fully capture patient-specific vascular risk, motivating a morphology-driven, uncertainty-aware learning approach.
AI.method: Carotid artery intima-media segmentation using AtheroFlow-XNet with an auxiliary risk-prediction branch incorporating clinical variables, and Monte Carlo dropout for uncertainty quantification. Trained/validated/tested on 1,522/326/328 images from CUBS dataset.
AI.result: Dice coefficient 0.7930 for LI-MA mask segmentation, segmentation loss 0.2359, and AUC of 0.6910 for risk prediction. Uncertainty maps highlighted ambiguous wall-boundary regions.
AI.conclusion: Ultrasound-derived carotid morphology can support automated wall analysis and uncertainty-aware interpretation, serving as a reproducible morphology-driven baseline. Future work will incorporate Doppler flow profiles, vascular reconstruction, and CFD-based biomarkers.

title: Evo-Depth: A Lightweight Depth-Enhanced Vision-Language-Action Model
summary: Vision-Language-Action models have emerged as a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation by unifying perception, language grounding, and action generation. However, they often struggle in scenarios requiring precise spatial understanding, as current VLA models primarily rely on 2D visual representations that lack depth information and detailed spatial relationships. While recent approaches incorporate explicit 3D inputs such as depth maps or point clouds to address this issue, they often increase system complexity, require additional sensors, and remain vulnerable to sensing noise and reconstruction errors. Another line of work explores implicit 3D-aware spatial modeling directly from RGB observations without extra sensors, but it often relies on large geometry foundation models, resulting in higher training and deployment costs. To address these challenges, we propose Evo-Depth, a lightweight depth-enhanced VLA framework that enhances spatially grounded manipulation without relying on additional sensing hardware or compromising deployment efficiency. Evo-Depth employs a lightweight Implicit Depth Encoding Module to extract compact depth features from multi-view RGB images. These features are incorporated into vision-language representations through a Spatial Enhancement Module via depth-aware modulation, enabling efficient spatial-semantic enhancement. A Progressive Alignment Training strategy is further introduced to align the resulting depth-enhanced representations with downstream action learning. With only 0.9B parameters, Evo-Depth achieves superior performance across four simulation benchmarks. In real-world experiments, Evo-Depth attains the highest average success rate while also exhibiting the smallest model size, lowest GPU memory usage, and highest inference frequency among compared methods.

AI.tldr: Evo-Depth is a lightweight depth-enhanced VLA framework (0.9B params) that improves spatial understanding in robotic manipulation by extracting implicit depth features from multi-view RGB images, without needing extra sensors or complex 3D models, achieving state-of-the-art performance across simulation and real-world benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models struggle with precise spatial understanding due to reliance on 2D visual representations lacking depth. Prior solutions using explicit 3D inputs (depth maps, point clouds) increase complexity and sensor costs, while implicit 3D methods rely on large geometry foundation models with high training/deployment costs.
AI.method: Evo-Depth uses (1) a lightweight Implicit Depth Encoding Module to extract compact depth features from multi-view RGB images, (2) a Spatial Enhancement Module via depth-aware modulation to integrate depth into vision-language representations, and (3) a Progressive Alignment Training strategy to align depth-enhanced representations with action learning.
AI.result: With only 0.9B parameters, Evo-Depth achieves superior performance across four simulation benchmarks. In real-world experiments, it attains the highest average success rate, smallest model size, lowest GPU memory usage, and highest inference frequency among compared methods.
AI.conclusion: Evo-Depth demonstrates that lightweight, implicit depth encoding from RGB inputs alone can effectively enhance spatial grounding in VLA models without extra sensors or high deployment costs, offering an efficient and practical solution for robotic manipulation.

title: H-OmniStereo: Zero-Shot Omnidirectional Stereo Matching with Heading-Aligned Normal Priors
summary: Stereo matching on top-bottom equirectangular images provides an effective framework for full-surround perception, as vertically aligned epipolar lines enable the use of advanced perspective stereo architectures that are largely driven by large-scale datasets and monocular priors. However, the performance of such adaptations is severely limited by the scarcity of omnidirectional stereo datasets and the degradation of perspective monocular priors under spherical distortions.To address these challenges, we propose H-OmniStereo, a zero-shot omnidirectional stereo matching framework. First, we construct high-quality synthetic dataset comprising over 2.8 million top-bottom equirectangular stereo pairs to scale up training. Second, we introduce an equirectangular monocular normal estimator, specifically operating in a heading-aligned coordinate system. Beyond providing distortion-robust and cross-view-consistent geometric priors for establishing reliable correspondences in stereo matching, this design boosts training efficiency and accommodates train-test FoV mismatches.Extensive experiments show that our approach achieves higher accuracy than existing methods on out-of-domain datasets and successfully generalizes to real-world consumer camera setups using a single model. Both the model and the dataset will be open-sourced.

AI.tldr: H-OmniStereo is a zero-shot omnidirectional stereo matching framework that addresses dataset scarcity and distortion challenges by introducing a 2.8M-pair synthetic dataset and an equirectangular monocular normal estimator, achieving superior generalization to out-of-domain and real-world data.
AI.motivation: Stereo matching on top-bottom equirectangular images enables full-surround perception, but performance is severely limited by the scarcity of omnidirectional stereo datasets and the degradation of perspective monocular priors under spherical distortions.
AI.method: (1) Constructing a high-quality synthetic dataset with 2.8M+ top-bottom equirectangular stereo pairs. (2) Introducing an equirectangular monocular normal estimator operating in a heading-aligned coordinate system, providing distortion-robust and cross-view-consistent geometric priors while boosting training efficiency and handling train-test FoV mismatches.
AI.result: Higher accuracy than existing methods on out-of-domain datasets and successful generalization to real-world consumer camera setups using a single model.
AI.conclusion: H-OmniStereo effectively addresses dataset scarcity and distortion degradation challenges in omnidirectional stereo matching, achieving strong zero-shot generalization. The model and dataset will be open-sourced.

title: MHSA: A Lightweight Framework for Mitigating Hallucinations via Steered Attention in LVLMs
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have achieved remarkable performance across diverse multimodal tasks, yet they continue to suffer from hallucinations, generating content that is inconsistent with the visual input. Prior work DHCP (Detecting Hallucinations by Cross-modal Attention Pattern) has explored hallucination detection from the perspective of cross-modal attention, but does not address hallucination mitigation. In this paper, we propose MHSA (Mitigating Hallucinations via Steered Attention), a lightweight framework that mitigates hallucinations by learning to correct cross-modal attention patterns in LVLMs. MHSA trains a simple three-layer MLP generator to produce corrected attention, guided by supervisory signals from the DHCP discriminator and the LVLM itself. During inference, MHSA mitigates both discriminative and generative hallucinations across various datasets and LVLMs by simply replacing the original cross-modal attention with the corrected one, without modifying any LVLM parameters. By extending cross-modal attention mechanisms from hallucination detection to hallucination mitigation, MHSA offers a novel perspective on hallucination research in LVLMs and helps enhance their reliability.

AI.tldr: MHSA is a lightweight framework that mitigates hallucinations in LVLMs by learning to correct cross-modal attention patterns using a three-layer MLP generator, guided by DHCP discriminator and LVLM signals, without modifying original model parameters.
AI.motivation: LVLMs suffer from hallucinations (generating content inconsistent with visual input). Prior work DHCP detected hallucinations via cross-modal attention but did not address mitigation. The authors aim to extend attention-based approaches from detection to mitigation.
AI.method: Trains a simple three-layer MLP generator to produce corrected cross-modal attention, guided by supervisory signals from the DHCP discriminator and the LVLM itself. During inference, the corrected attention replaces the original cross-modal attention without modifying any LVLM parameters.
AI.result: MHSA mitigates both discriminative and generative hallucinations across various datasets and LVLMs.
AI.conclusion: Extending cross-modal attention mechanisms from hallucination detection to mitigation offers a novel perspective and enhances the reliability of LVLMs.

title: MicroscopyMatching: Towards a Ready-to-use Framework for Microscopy Image Analysis in Diverse Conditions
summary: Analyzing microscopy images to extract biological object properties (e.g., their morphological organization, temporal dynamics, and population density) is fundamental to various biomedical research. Yet conducting this manually is costly and time-consuming. Though deep learning-based approaches have been explored to automate this process, the substantial diversity of microscopy analysis settings in practice (including variations of biological object types, sample processing protocols, imaging equipment, and analysis tasks, etc.) often renders them ineffective. As a result, these approaches typically require extensive adaptation for different settings, which, however, can impose burdens that are often practically unsustainable for laboratories, forcing biomedical researchers to still commonly rely on manual analysis, thereby severely bottlenecking the pace of biomedical research progress. This situation has created a pressing and long-standing need for a reliable and broadly applicable microscopy image analysis tool, yet such a tool is still missing. To address this gap, we present the first ready-to-use microscopy image analysis framework, MicroscopyMatching, that can reliably perform key analysis tasks (including segmentation, tracking, and counting) across diverse microscopy analysis settings. From a fundamentally different perspective, MicroscopyMatching reformulates diverse microscopy image analysis tasks as a unified matching problem, effectively handling this problem by exploiting the robust matching capability from pre-trained latent diffusion models.

AI.tldr: MicroscopyMatching reformulates diverse microscopy image analysis tasks (segmentation, tracking, counting) into a unified matching problem using pre-trained latent diffusion models, offering a ready-to-use framework that works across various microscopy settings without requiring extensive adaptation.
AI.motivation: Manual analysis of microscopy images is costly and time-consuming; existing deep learning approaches fail due to the diversity of microscopy settings and require extensive adaptation that is unsustainable for labs, creating a need for a reliable, broadly applicable tool that is currently missing.
AI.method: Reformulates diverse microscopy analysis tasks (segmentation, tracking, counting) as a unified matching problem and leverages the robust matching capability from pre-trained latent diffusion models to solve this problem across different settings.
AI.result: A ready-to-use microscopy image analysis framework called MicroscopyMatching that can reliably perform segmentation, tracking, and counting across diverse microscopy analysis settings.
AI.conclusion: MicroscopyMatching addresses the long-standing gap in reliable, broadly applicable microscopy image analysis by unifying diverse tasks under a matching problem framework and exploiting latent diffusion models, potentially eliminating the bottleneck in biomedical research progress caused by manual analysis.

title: Sat3DGen: Comprehensive Street-Level 3D Scene Generation from Single Satellite Image
summary: Generating a street-level 3D scene from a single satellite image is a crucial yet challenging task. Current methods present a stark trade-off: geometry-colorization models achieve high geometric fidelity but are typically building-focused and lack semantic diversity. In contrast, proxy-based models use feed-forward image-to-3D frameworks to generate holistic scenes by jointly learning geometry and texture, a process that yields rich content but coarse and unstable geometry. We attribute these geometric failures to the extreme viewpoint gap and sparse, inconsistent supervision inherent in satellite-to-street data. We introduce Sat3DGen to address these fundamental challenges, which embodies a geometry-first methodology. This methodology enhances the feed-forward paradigm by integrating novel geometric constraints with a perspective-view training strategy, explicitly countering the primary sources of geometric error. This geometry-centric strategy yields a dramatic leap in both 3D accuracy and photorealism. For validation, we first constructed a new benchmark by pairing the VIGOR-OOD test set with high-resolution DSM data. On this benchmark, our method improves geometric RMSE from 6.76m to 5.20m. Crucially, this geometric leap also boosts photorealism, reducing the Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) from $\sim$40 to 19 against the leading method, Sat2Density++, despite using no extra tailored image-quality modules. We demonstrate the versatility of our high-quality 3D assets through diverse downstream applications, including semantic-map-to-3D synthesis, multi-camera video generation, large-scale meshing, and unsupervised single-image Digital Surface Model (DSM) estimation. The code has been released on https://github.com/qianmingduowan/Sat3DGen.

AI.tldr: Sat3DGen introduces a geometry-first methodology for generating street-level 3D scenes from single satellite images, significantly improving both geometric accuracy (RMSE from 6.76m to 5.20m) and photorealism (FID from ~40 to 19) over prior methods, with demonstrated versatility across multiple downstream applications.
AI.motivation: Current satellite-to-street 3D generation methods face a trade-off: geometry-colorization models achieve high geometric fidelity but are building-focused and lack semantic diversity, while proxy-based models produce richer content but suffer from coarse and unstable geometry due to extreme viewpoint gaps and sparse, inconsistent supervision in satellite-to-street data.
AI.method: Sat3DGen employs a geometry-first methodology that enhances feed-forward image-to-3D frameworks by integrating novel geometric constraints with a perspective-view training strategy, explicitly addressing the primary sources of geometric error caused by viewpoint gaps and inconsistent supervision.
AI.result: On a new benchmark pairing VIGOR-OOD test set with high-resolution DSM data, Sat3DGen improves geometric RMSE from 6.76m to 5.20m. It also reduces FID from ~40 to 19 against Sat2Density++, without using extra image-quality modules. The method is demonstrated on semantic-map-to-3D synthesis, multi-camera video generation, large-scale meshing, and unsupervised single-image DSM estimation.
AI.conclusion: The geometry-centric strategy yields a dramatic leap in both 3D accuracy and photorealism, and the high-quality 3D assets show versatility across diverse downstream applications. Code is publicly released.

title: Compositional Video Generation via Inference-Time Guidance
summary: Text-to-video diffusion models generate realistic videos, but often fail on prompts requiring fine-grained compositional understanding, such as relations between entities, attributes, actions, and motion directions. We hypothesize that these failures need not be addressed by retraining the generator, but can instead be mitigated by steering the denoising process using the model's own internal grounding signals. We propose \textbf{CVG}, an inference-time guidance method for improving compositional faithfulness in frozen text-to-video models. Our key observation is that cross-attention maps already encode how prompt concepts are grounded across space and time. We train a lightweight compositional classifier on these attention features and use its gradients during early denoising steps to steer the latent trajectory toward the desired composition. Built on a frozen VLM backbone, the classifier transfers across semantically related composition labels rather than relying only on narrow category-specific features. CVG improves compositional generation without modifying the model architecture, fine-tuning the generator, or requiring layouts, boxes, or other user-supplied controls. Experiments on compositional text-to-video benchmarks show improved prompt faithfulness while preserving the visual quality of the underlying generator.

AI.tldr: CVG is an inference-time guidance method that leverages cross-attention maps from frozen text-to-video models and a lightweight classifier to steer denoising toward better compositional faithfulness without retraining or architectural changes.
AI.motivation: Text-to-video diffusion models fail on prompts requiring fine-grained compositional understanding (e.g., relations, attributes, actions, motion directions). The authors hypothesize these failures can be fixed at inference-time without retraining the generator.
AI.method: CVG uses cross-attention maps (which encode how prompt concepts are grounded across space and time) as input to a lightweight compositional classifier trained on a frozen VLM backbone. During early denoising steps, gradients from this classifier steer the latent trajectory toward desired compositions.
AI.result: CVG improves compositional faithfulness on compositional text-to-video benchmarks while preserving the visual quality of the underlying generator.
AI.conclusion: Compositional failures in pretrained text-to-video models can be effectively mitigated via inference-time guidance using the model's own internal grounding signals, without modifying architecture, fine-tuning, or requiring user-provided controls.

title: Characterizing the visual representation of objects from the child's view
summary: Children acquire object category representations from their everyday experiences in the first few years of life. What do the inputs to this learning process look like? We analyzed first-person videos of young children's visual experience at home from the BabyView dataset ($N$ = 31 participants, 868 hours, ages 5--36 months), using a supervised object detection model to extract common object categories from more than 3 million frames. We found that children's object category exposure was highly skewed: a few categories (e.g., cups, chairs) dominated children's visual experiences while most categories appeared rarely, replicating previous findings from a more restricted set of contexts. Category exemplars were highly variable: children encountered objects from unusual angles, in highly cluttered scenes, and partially occluded views; many categories (especially animals) were most frequently viewed as depictions. Surprisingly, despite this variability, detected categories (e.g., giraffes, apples) showed stronger groupings within superordinate categories (e.g., animals, food) relative to groupings derived from canonical photographs of these categories. We found this same pattern when using high-dimensional embeddings from both self-supervised visual and multimodal models; this effect was also recapitulated in densely sampled data from individual children. Understanding the robustness and efficiency of visual category learning will require the development of models that can exploit strong superordinate structure and learn from non-canonical, sparse, and variable exemplars.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes 868 hours of first-person video from 31 children (5-36 months) to understand the visual inputs for object category learning, finding highly skewed category distributions, highly variable exemplars, but surprisingly strong superordinate category structure even stronger than canonical photographs.
AI.motivation: To characterize the real-world visual input children receive during early category learning, moving beyond laboratory studies to understand what naturalistic learning environments actually look like.
AI.method: Analyzed 868 hours of first-person video from 31 children (ages 5-36 months) using a supervised object detection model on 3+ million frames from the BabyView dataset.
AI.result: (1) Category exposure is highly skewed (a few categories dominate). (2) Exemplars are highly variable (unusual angles, cluttered scenes, occluded views; animals often seen as depictions). (3) Despite variability, detected categories show stronger superordinate groupings (e.g., animals, food) than canonical photographs, replicating with self-supervised and multimodal models and in individual children's data.
AI.conclusion: Understanding visual category learning requires models that exploit strong superordinate structure and learn from non-canonical, sparse, and variable exemplars.

title: Predicting Response to Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy in Ovarian Cancer from CT Baseline Using Multi-Loss Deep Learning
summary: Ovarian cancer is the most lethal gynecologic malignancy: around 60% of patients are diagnosed at an advanced stage, with an associated 5-year survival rate of about 30%. Early identification of non-responders to neoadjuvant chemotherapy remains a key unmet need, as it could prevent ineffective therapy and avoid delays in optimal surgical management. This work proposes a non-invasive deep learning framework to predict neoadjuvant chemotherapy response from pre-treatment contrast-enhanced CT by leveraging automatically derived 3D lesion masks. The approach encodes axial slices with a partially fine-tuned pretrained image encoder and aggregates slice-level representations into a volumetric embedding through an attention-based module. Training combines classification loss with supervised contrastive regularization and hard-negative mining to improve separation between ambiguous responders and non-responders. The method was developed on a retrospective single-center cohort from the European Institute of Oncology (Milan, IT), including 280 eligible patients (147 responder, 133 non-responder). On the test cohort, the model achieved a ROC-AUC of 0.73 (95% CI: 0.58-0.86) and an F1-score of 0.70 (95% CI: 0.56-0.82). Overall, these results suggest that the proposed architecture learns clinically relevant predictive patterns and provides a robust foundation for an imaging-based stratification tool.

AI.tldr: A deep learning framework using pre-treatment CT scans predicts chemotherapy response in ovarian cancer patients, achieving ROC-AUC of 0.73 and F1-score of 0.70 on a retrospective cohort of 280 patients.
AI.motivation: Early identification of non-responders to neoadjuvant chemotherapy in ovarian cancer is an unmet need, as it could prevent ineffective therapy and avoid delays in optimal surgical management.
AI.method: The approach uses a partially fine-tuned pretrained image encoder on axial CT slices, aggregates slice-level representations via attention-based module, and combines classification loss with supervised contrastive regularization and hard-negative mining.
AI.result: On the test cohort (280 patients; 147 responder, 133 non-responder), the model achieved ROC-AUC of 0.73 (95% CI: 0.58-0.86) and F1-score of 0.70 (95% CI: 0.56-0.82).
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture learns clinically relevant predictive patterns and provides a robust foundation for an imaging-based stratification tool.

title: 3D Skew-Normal Splatting
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a leading representation for real-time novel view synthesis and been widely adopted in various downstream applications. The core strength of 3DGS lies in its efficient kernel-based scene representation, where Gaussian primitives provide favorable mathematical and computational properties. However, under a finite primitive budget, the symmetric shape of each primitive directly affects representation compactness, especially near asymmetric structures such as object boundaries and one-sided surfaces. Recent works have explored more complex kernel distributions, yet they either remain within the elliptical family or rely on hard truncation, which limits continuous shape control and introduces distributional discontinuities. In this paper, we propose Skew-Normal Splatting (SNS), which adopts the Azzalini Skew-Normal distribution as the fundamental primitive. By introducing a learnable and bounded skewness parameter, SNS can continuously interpolate between symmetric Gaussians and Half-Gaussian-like shapes, enabling flexible modeling of both sharp boundaries and interior regions. Moremover, SNS preserves analytical tractability under affine transformations and marginalization. This property allows seamless integration into existing Gaussian Splatting rasterization pipelines.Furthermore, to address the strong coupling between scale, rotation, and skewness parameters, we introduce a decoupled parameterization and a block-wise optimization strategy to enhance training stability and accuracy. Extensive experiments on standard novel-view synthesis benchmarks show that SNS consistently improves reconstruction quality over Gaussian and recent non-Gaussian kernels, with clearer benefits on sharp boundaries and thin or one-sided structures.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Skew-Normal Splatting (SNS), which uses Azzalini Skew-Normal distributions as primitives in 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling continuous interpolation between symmetric Gaussian and Half-Gaussian-like shapes for better representation of asymmetric structures like object boundaries.
AI.motivation: Under finite primitive budgets, the symmetric shape of Gaussian primitives limits representation compactness near asymmetric structures such as object boundaries and one-sided surfaces. Existing non-Gaussian kernels either remain in the elliptical family or use hard truncation, which limits continuous shape control.
AI.method: Proposes Skew-Normal Splatting (SNS) using Azzalini Skew-Normal distribution as primitives with a learnable bounded skewness parameter for continuous shape control, plus a decoupled parameterization and block-wise optimization strategy to handle coupling between scale, rotation, and skewness.
AI.result: SNS consistently improves reconstruction quality over Gaussian and recent non-Gaussian kernels on standard novel-view synthesis benchmarks, with clearer benefits on sharp boundaries and thin or one-sided structures.
AI.conclusion: Skew-Normal Splatting provides a more flexible primitive representation that preserves analytical tractability and integrates seamlessly into existing 3DGS pipelines while improving reconstruction quality.

title: HiSem: Hierarchical Semantic Disentangling for Remote Sensing Image Change Captioning
summary: Remote sensing image change captioning (RSICC) aims to achieve high-level semantic understanding of genuine changes occurring between bi-temporal images. Despite notable progress, existing methods are fundamentally limited by a shared modeling assumption: changed and unchanged image pairs, which have intrinsically different semantic granularities, are processed under a unified modeling strategy. This modeling inconsistency leads to semantic entanglement between coarse-grained change existence judgment and fine-grained semantic understanding.To address the above limitation, we propose a novel hierarchical semantic disentangling network (HiSem) that explicitly disentangles semantic representations of different granularities. Specifically, we first introduce the Bidirectional Differential Attention Modulation (BDAM) module that leverages discrepancy-aware attention to enhance cross-temporal interactions, thereby amplifying true change signals while suppressing irrelevant variations. Building upon this, we design a Hierarchical Adaptive Semantic Disentanglement (HASD) module that performs adaptive routing at two hierarchical levels: a coarse-grained image-level routing mechanism distinguishes changed and unchanged image pairs, while a fine-grained token-level Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) block models diverse and heterogeneous change semantics for changed samples. Extensive experiments on two benchmark datasets demonstrate that HiSem outperfoms previous methods, achieving a significant improvement of +7.52\% BLEU-4 on the WHU-CDC dataset. More importantly, our approach provides a structured perspective for RSICC by explicitly aligning model design with the intrinsic semantic heterogeneity of bi-temporal scenes. The code will be available at https://github.com/Man-Wang-star/HiSem

AI.tldr: HiSem is a hierarchical semantic disentangling network for remote sensing image change captioning that distinguishes coarse-grained image-level routing (changed vs. unchanged) from fine-grained token-level MoE modeling, achieving +7.52% BLEU-4 on WHU-CDC.
AI.motivation: Existing RSICC methods suffer from semantic entanglement because they use a unified modeling strategy for both changed and unchanged image pairs, which have intrinsically different semantic granularities.
AI.method: HiSem consists of two key modules: (1) Bidirectional Differential Attention Modulation (BDAM) for enhancing cross-temporal change signals, and (2) Hierarchical Adaptive Semantic Disentanglement (HASD) with coarse-grained image-level routing and fine-grained token-level Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) for change semantics.
AI.result: Outperforms previous methods with a significant +7.52% BLEU-4 improvement on the WHU-CDC dataset.
AI.conclusion: Provides a structured perspective for RSICC by aligning model design with the intrinsic semantic heterogeneity of bi-temporal scenes.

title: EverAnimate: Minute-Scale Human Animation via Latent Flow Restoration
summary: We propose EverAnimate, an efficient post-training method for long-horizon animated video generation that preserves visual quality and character identity. Long-form animation remains challenging because highly dynamic human motion must be synthesized against relatively static environments, making chunk-based generation prone to accumulated drift: (i) low-level quality drift, such as progressive degradation of static backgrounds, and (ii) high-level semantic drift, such as inconsistent character identity and view-dependent attributes. To address this issue, EverAnimate restores drifted flow trajectories by anchoring generation to a persistent latent context memory, consisting of two complementary mechanisms. (i) Persistent Latent Propagation maintains a context memory across chunks to propagate identity and motion in latent space while mitigating temporal forgetting. (ii) Restorative Flow Matching introduces an implicit restoration objective during sampling through velocity adjustment, improving within-chunk fidelity. With only lightweight LoRA tuning, EverAnimate outperforms state-of-the-art long-animation methods in both short- and long-horizon settings: at 10 seconds, it improves PSNR/SSIM by 8%/7% and reduces LPIPS/FID by 22%/11%; at 90 seconds, the gains increase to 15%/15% and 32%/27%, respectively.

AI.tldr: EverAnimate is a post-training method for long-horizon animated video generation that uses two mechanisms—Persistent Latent Propagation and Restorative Flow Matching—to prevent quality and identity drift, achieving significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods at both short (10s) and long (90s) durations.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon animated video generation suffers from accumulated drift in both low-level quality (e.g., background degradation) and high-level semantics (e.g., inconsistent character identity and view-dependent attributes) when using chunk-based generation for highly dynamic human motion against static environments.
AI.method: EverAnimate employs two complementary mechanisms: (1) Persistent Latent Propagation, which maintains a context memory across chunks to propagate identity and motion in latent space while mitigating temporal forgetting, and (2) Restorative Flow Matching, which introduces an implicit restoration objective during sampling through velocity adjustment to improve within-chunk fidelity. The method uses lightweight LoRA tuning.
AI.result: At 10 seconds, EverAnimate improves PSNR/SSIM by 8%/7% and reduces LPIPS/FID by 22%/11%. At 90 seconds, the gains increase to 15%/15% and 32%/27%, respectively, outperforming state-of-the-art long-animation methods in both short- and long-horizon settings.
AI.conclusion: EverAnimate effectively addresses drift in long-horizon animated video generation through persistent latent memory and restorative flow matching, with greater relative improvements at longer horizons, demonstrating its suitability for long-form animation tasks.

title: LATERN: Test-Time Context-Aware Explainable Video Anomaly Detection
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for video anomaly detection (VAD) due to their strong visual reasoning ability and natural language-based explainability. In this paper, we aim to address a key limitation of such pipelines, which perform segment-level inference independently owing to token constraints and reason without structured temporal context, allowing VLMs to interpret anomalies as deviations from evolving video dynamics rather than producing fragmented predictions and explanations. To specify, we propose a context-aware framework named LATERN, which reformulates VAD as a temporal evidence aggregation process. LATERN consists of two complementary modules: Context-Aware Anomaly Scoring (CEA) and Recursive Evidence Aggregation (REA). CEA introduces a novel image-grounded memory mechanism, which selectively chooses historical content via frame diversity and visual-textual alignment as expanded context to help generate reliable anomaly scores. Building upon these scores, REA performs recursive temporal aggregation to identify coherent anomaly intervals and produce event-level decisions and explanations grounded in visual-textual evidence. Extensive experiments on challenging benchmarks, including UCF-Crime and XD-Violence, show that LATERN enhances detection accuracy and explanation consistency for frozen VLMs during test time, while generating temporally coherent and semantically grounded event-level explanations.

AI.tldr: LATERN is a context-aware framework that reformulates video anomaly detection as temporal evidence aggregation, using two modules (CEA and REA) to enable VLMs to produce temporally coherent, event-level anomaly scores and explanations by considering evolving video dynamics rather than fragmented segment-level inference.
AI.motivation: Current VLMs for VAD perform segment-level inference independently due to token constraints and reason without structured temporal context, leading to fragmented predictions and explanations. The authors aim to let VLMs interpret anomalies as deviations from evolving video dynamics.
AI.method: LATERN consists of two complementary modules: (1) Context-Aware Anomaly Scoring (CEA) with an image-grounded memory mechanism that selectively chooses historical content via frame diversity and visual-textual alignment as expanded context for reliable anomaly scores, and (2) Recursive Evidence Aggregation (REA) that performs temporal aggregation to identify coherent anomaly intervals and produce event-level decisions and explanations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on UCF-Crime and XD-Violence benchmarks show LATERN enhances detection accuracy and explanation consistency for frozen VLMs during test time, while generating temporally coherent and semantically grounded event-level explanations.
AI.conclusion: Addressing temporal context limitations enables VLMs to produce more coherent and semantically grounded event-level anomaly detection and explanations compared to segment-level inference.

title: Computational Imaging Priors for Wireless Capsule Endoscopy: Monte Carlo-Guided Hemoglobin Mapping for Rare-Anomaly Detection
comment: 24 pages, 6 figures, 3 tables. Code and trained-model checkpoints at https://github.com/integritynoble/GI_Multi_Task . 6-seed (seeds 41, 42, 43, 44, 45, 47) mean +/- SD ablation as the headline; per-class single-seed=42 analyses in Appendix A
summary:
Background. RGB-trained capsule-endoscopy classifiers underperform on small-vessel vascular findings by
  conflating hemoglobin contrast with bile and illumination falloff. Thus, here we test whether a Monte
  Carlo-inspired analytic model can compute hemoglobin from RGB signal built upon extracted classifier.
  Methods. On Kvasir-Capsule (47,238 frames, video-level 70/15/15 split, 11 evaluable classes) we evaluate two
  software-only configurations against RGB-only EfficientNet-B0 across 6 seeds: (i) a prior P_blood =
  sigma(alpha * (H_norm - 0.5)) * Phi(r) fused as 2 zero-init auxiliary channels; (ii) a distillation head
  training a 3-channel RGB backbone to predict P_blood. Significance: paired DeLong, McNemar, bootstrap CIs
  with Bonferroni correction.
  Results. Across 6 seeds (n=6,423), the analytic prior provides a small but direction-consistent macro-AUC
  improvement: RGB-only 0.760 +/- 0.027, input-fusion 0.783 +/- 0.024 (paired Delta = +0.023, sign-positive on
  5/6 seeds), distillation 0.773 +/- 0.028. The largest robust per-class lift is on Lymphangiectasia, where AUC
  rises from RGB 0.238 +/- 0.057 to input-fusion 0.337 +/- 0.019, sign-consistent across all 6 seeds. On rare
  focal-vascular classes (Angiectasia, Blood - fresh) the prior's per-seed effects are bimodal: seed=42 reaches
  Angiectasia AUC 0.528 -> 0.916, but the cross-seed mean is 0.646 -> 0.608 with sigma_PI = 0.23 - reported as
  a high-variance per-seed exemplar.
  Conclusion. A Monte Carlo-inspired analytic prior provides a small, direction-consistent macro-AUC
  improvement on Kvasir-Capsule across 6 seeds with the largest robust per-class lift on Lymphangiectasia; the
  distillation variant runs on plain 3-channel RGB and yields a free interpretability heatmap.

AI.tldr: A Monte Carlo-inspired analytic model computing hemoglobin from RGB signals provides a small but consistent macro-AUC improvement (+0.023) over RGB-only baseline on Kvasir-Capsule, with the largest robust gain on Lymphangiectasia (AUC 0.238→0.337).
AI.motivation: RGB-trained capsule-endoscopy classifiers conflate hemoglobin contrast with bile and illumination falloff, causing underperformance on small-vessel vascular findings.
AI.method: Evaluated two configurations against RGB-only EfficientNet-B0 on Kvasir-Capsule (47,238 frames, 11 classes, 6 seeds): (i) input-fusion with a Monte Carlo-inspired analytic prior P_blood as auxiliary channels; (ii) distillation head training a 3-channel RGB backbone to predict P_blood. Significance testing used paired DeLong, McNemar, bootstrap CIs with Bonferroni correction.
AI.result: Input-fusion achieved macro-AUC 0.783 vs RGB-only 0.760 (Delta=+0.023, sign-positive on 5/6 seeds). Lymphangiectasia AUC rose from 0.238 to 0.337 (consistent across all seeds). Rare vascular classes showed bimodal results (e.g., seed=42 Angiectasia AUC 0.528→0.916, but cross-seed mean decreased).
AI.conclusion: The analytic prior provides a small, direction-consistent improvement; distillation variant runs on plain 3-channel RGB with an interpretability heatmap benefit.

title: On the Cultural Anachronism and Temporal Reasoning in Vision Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are increasingly applied to cultural heritage materials, from digital archives to educational platforms. This work identifies a fundamental issue in how these models interpret historical artifacts. We define this phenomenon as cultural anachronism, the tendency to misinterpret historical objects using temporally inappropriate concepts, materials, or cultural frameworks. To quantify this phenomenon, we introduce the Temporal Anachronism Benchmark for Vision-Language Models (TAB-VLM), a dataset of 600 questions across six categories, designed to evaluate temporal reasoning on 1,600 Indian cultural artifacts spanning prehistoric to modern periods. Systematic evaluations of ten state-of-the-art models reveal significant deficiencies on our benchmark, and even the best model (GPT-5.2) achieves only 58.7% overall accuracy. The performance gap persists across varying architectures and scales, suggesting that cultural anachronism represents a significant limitation in visual AI systems, regardless of model size. These findings highlight the disparity between current VLM capabilities and the requirements for accurately interpreting cultural heritage materials, particularly for non-Western visual cultures underrepresented in training data. Our benchmark provides a foundation for enhancing temporal cognition in multimodal AI systems that interact with historical artifacts. The dataset and code are available in our project page.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies "cultural anachronism" in VLMs—their tendency to misinterpret historical artifacts using temporally inappropriate concepts. The authors introduce TAB-VLM, a 600-question benchmark across six categories testing temporal reasoning on 1,600 Indian artifacts. Evaluating ten state-of-the-art models, the best (GPT-5.2) achieves only 58.7% accuracy, revealing significant limitations regardless of model size or architecture.
AI.motivation: VLMs are increasingly applied to cultural heritage, but no existing work systematically evaluates their tendency to misinterpret historical objects through temporally inappropriate concepts, especially for non-Western visual cultures underrepresented in training data.
AI.method: The authors define "cultural anachronism" and construct the Temporal Anachronism Benchmark for Vision-Language Models (TAB-VLM)—a dataset of 600 questions across six categories covering 1,600 Indian cultural artifacts from prehistoric to modern periods. They systematically evaluate ten state-of-the-art VLMs on this benchmark.
AI.result: The best model (GPT-5.2) achieves only 58.7% overall accuracy. Performance gaps persist across varying architectures and scales, indicating cultural anachronism is a fundamental limitation regardless of model size.
AI.conclusion: Current VLM capabilities are insufficient for accurately interpreting cultural heritage materials, particularly for non-Western visual cultures. The benchmark provides a foundation for improving temporal cognition in multimodal AI systems.

title: SAGE3D: Soft-guided attention and graph excitation for 3D point cloud corner detection
summary: We present SAGE3D, a hybrid Transformer-based model for corner detection in airborne LiDAR point clouds. We propose a multi-stage solution built on a hierarchical encoder-decoder architecture that progressively downsamples point clouds through Set Abstraction layers and recovers per-point predictions via Feature Propagation. We introduce two innovations: Soft-Guided Attention, which injects ground-truth corner labels as a log-prior into attention logits during training to improve precision; then an Excitatory Graph Neural Network positioned at strategic resolutions in the hierarchy, employing positive-only message passing where high-confidence corners reinforce predictions through learned boosting, optimizing for recall. The hierarchical design enables multi-scale feature extraction while our guided attention and excitatory modules ensure corner signals are amplified rather than diluted across scales.

AI.tldr: SAGE3D is a hybrid Transformer model for corner detection in LiDAR point clouds, using Soft-Guided Attention and Excitatory GNN to improve precision and recall.
AI.motivation: To improve corner detection in airborne LiDAR point clouds by preventing corner signals from being diluted across scales in hierarchical models.
AI.method: Hybrid Transformer with hierarchical encoder-decoder (Set Abstraction + Feature Propagation), Soft-Guided Attention (ground-truth log-prior injection), and Excitatory GNN (positive-only message passing for boosting high-confidence corners).
AI.result: Precision improved via Soft-Guided Attention; recall improved via Excitatory GNN with positive-only message passing.
AI.conclusion: The proposed guided attention and excitatory modules successfully amplify corner signals rather than diluting them across multi-scale hierarchy.

title: CoralLite: μCT Reconstruction of Coral Colonies from Individual Corallites
summary: The life history of an individual coral is archived within the accreting skeleton of the colony. While reef-forming coral colonies (e.g. massive \emph{Porites} sp.) may live for hundreds of years and deposit calcareous structures many metres in height and width, their living tissue is a thin outer surface layer comprised of asexually-dividing polyps that only survive a few years. To understand the rate and timing of polyp division and the consequences for colony skeletal growth, scientists need to track the skeletal corallite deposited around each polyp. Here we propose CoralLite, an annotated μCT scan dataset of entire calcareous skeletons and an associated, first corallite deep learning reconstruction baseline. CoralLite combines fully quantified volumetric segmentations with cross-slice linking for visualisations of 3D models for each corallite up to colony scale. For segmentation, we propose and evaluate in detail a hybrid V-Trans-UNet architecture applicable to segmenting tiled μCT virtual slabs of \emph{Porites} sp. colonies. The model is pre-trained on weakly annotated data and topology-aware fine-tuned using fully annotated slice sections with 8k+ manual corallite region annotations. On unseen slices of the same colony, the resulting model reaches 0.94 topological accuracy at mean Dice scores of 0.77 on the same colony and projection axis, and 0.63 mean Dice scores on a different, biologically unrelated specimen. Whilst our experiments are limited in scale and context, our results show for the first time that visual machine learning can effectively support full 3D individual corallite modelling from μCT scans of coral skeletons alone. For reproducibility and as a baseline for future research we publish our full dataset of 697 μCT slices, 37 partial or full slice annotations, and all network weights and source code with this paper.

AI.tldr: CoralLite is a dataset and deep learning baseline (hybrid V-Trans-UNet) for segmenting individual corallites from μCT scans of coral skeletons, enabling 3D reconstruction of thousands of corallites per colony with topological accuracy up to 0.94 and Dice scores up to 0.77 on the same colony.
AI.motivation: To understand the rate and timing of polyp division and consequences for colony skeletal growth, scientists need to track the skeletal corallite deposited around each polyp, but no automated method existed for full 3D individual corallite modeling from μCT scans of coral skeletons.
AI.method: CoralLite dataset (697 μCT slices, 8k+ manual corallite annotations) + hybrid V-Trans-UNet architecture for tiled μCT virtual slab segmentation, pre-trained on weakly annotated data and topology-aware fine-tuned on fully annotated slices.
AI.result: 0.94 topological accuracy, 0.77 mean Dice (same colony, same axis), 0.63 mean Dice (different unrelated specimen).
AI.conclusion: Visual machine learning can effectively support full 3D individual corallite modeling from μCT scans of coral skeletons alone, establishing a baseline for future research.

title: DriveCtrl: Conditioned Sim-to-Real Driving Video Generation
summary: Large-scale labelled driving video data is essential for training autonomous driving systems. Although simulation offers scalable and fully annotated data, the domain gap between synthetic and real-world driving videos significantly limits its utility for downstream deployment. Existing video generation methods are not well-suited for this task, as they fail to simultaneously preserve scene structure, object dynamics, temporal consistency, and visual realism, all of which are critical for maintaining annotation validity in generated data. In this paper, we present DriveCtrl, a depth-conditioned controllable sim-to-real video generation framework for realistic driving video synthesis. Built upon a pretrained video foundation model, DriveCtrl introduces a structure-aware adapter that enables depth-guided generation while preserving the scene layout and motion patterns of the source simulation, producing temporally coherent driving videos that remain aligned with the original simulated sequences. We further introduce a scalable data generation pipeline that transforms simulator videos into realistic driving footage matching the visual style of a target real-world dataset. The pipeline supports three conditioning signals: structural depth, reference-dataset style, and text prompts, while preserving frame-level annotations for downstream perception tasks. To better assess this task, we propose a driving-domain-specific knowledge-informed evaluation metric called Driving Video Realism Score (DVRS) that assesses the realism of generated videos. Experiments demonstrate that DriveCtrl consistently outperforms the base model and competing alternatives in realism, temporal quality, and perception task performance, substantially narrowing the sim-to-real gap for driving video generation.

AI.tldr: DriveCtrl is a depth-conditioned controllable sim-to-real video generation framework that transforms synthetic driving videos into realistic footage while preserving scene structure, object dynamics, temporal consistency, and annotation validity. It introduces a structure-aware adapter, a scalable data pipeline with multiple conditioning signals, and a novel evaluation metric (DVRS) to bridge the sim-to-real gap for autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: Large-scale labelled driving video data is essential for training autonomous driving systems. Simulation provides scalable and fully annotated data, but the domain gap between synthetic and real-world videos limits its utility. Existing video generation methods fail to simultaneously preserve scene structure, object dynamics, temporal consistency, and visual realism needed for maintaining annotation validity.
AI.method: DriveCtrl is built upon a pretrained video foundation model with a structure-aware adapter for depth-guided generation. It uses a scalable data pipeline that transforms simulator videos into realistic footage with three conditioning signals: structural depth, reference-dataset style, and text prompts. The authors also propose DVRS (Driving Video Realism Score) as a domain-specific evaluation metric.
AI.result: DriveCtrl consistently outperforms the base model and competing alternatives in realism, temporal quality, and perception task performance, substantially narrowing the sim-to-real gap for driving video generation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively bridges the sim-to-real gap for driving video generation, enabling the creation of realistic, annotation-preserving driving footage from synthetic sources, which is critical for training autonomous driving systems.

title: MemEye: A Visual-Centric Evaluation Framework for Multimodal Agent Memory
summary: Long-term agent memory is increasingly multimodal, yet existing evaluations rarely test whether agents preserve the visual evidence needed for later reasoning. In prior work, many visually grounded questions can be answered using only captions or textual traces, allowing answers to be inferred without preserving the fine-grained visual evidence. Meanwhile, harder cases that require reasoning over changing visual states are largely absent. Therefore, we introduce MemEye, a framework that evaluates memory capabilities from two dimensions: one measures the granularity of decisive visual evidence (from scene-level to pixel-level evidence), and the other measures how retrieved evidence must be used (from single evidence to evolutionary synthesis). Under this framework, we construct a new benchmark across 8 life-scenario tasks, with ablation-driven validation gates for assessing answerability, shortcut resistance, visual necessity, and reasoning structure. By evaluating 13 memory methods across 4 VLM backbones, we show that current architectures still struggle to preserve fine-grained visual details and reason about state changes over time. Our findings show that long-term multimodal memory depends on evidence routing, temporal tracking, and detail extraction.

AI.tldr: MemEye evaluates long-term agent memory by testing the preservation of visual evidence across granularity levels (scene-to-pixel) and reasoning complexity (single evidence to evolutionary synthesis), finding current methods struggle with fine-grained visual details and temporal state changes.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of long-term agent memory rarely test whether agents preserve visual evidence for later reasoning, as many visually-grounded questions can be answered using only captions or textual traces, and harder cases requiring reasoning over changing visual states are absent.
AI.method: The paper introduces MemEye, a framework evaluating memory capabilities across two dimensions: (1) granularity of decisive visual evidence (scene-level to pixel-level), and (2) how retrieved evidence must be used (single evidence to evolutionary synthesis). They construct a benchmark across 8 life-scenario tasks with ablation-driven validation gates.
AI.result: Evaluation of 13 memory methods across 4 VLM backbones shows current architectures struggle to preserve fine-grained visual details and reason about state changes over time.
AI.conclusion: Long-term multimodal memory depends on evidence routing, temporal tracking, and detail extraction; current methods are insufficient for fine-grained visual reasoning over time.

title: Causal Forcing++: Scalable Few-Step Autoregressive Diffusion Distillation for Real-Time Interactive Video Generation
summary: Real-time interactive video generation requires low-latency, streaming, and controllable rollout. Existing autoregressive (AR) diffusion distillation methods have achieved strong results in the chunk-wise 4-step regime by distilling bidirectional base models into few-step AR students, but they remain limited by coarse response granularity and non-negligible sampling latency. In this paper, we study a more aggressive setting: frame-wise autoregression with only 1--2 sampling steps. In this regime, we identify the initialization of a few-step AR student as the key bottleneck: existing strategies are either target-misaligned, incapable of few-step generation, or too costly to scale. We propose \textbf{Causal Forcing++}, a principled and scalable pipeline that uses \emph{causal consistency distillation} (causal CD) for few-step AR initialization. The core idea is that causal CD learns the same AR-conditional flow map as causal ODE distillation, but obtains supervision from a single online teacher ODE step between adjacent timesteps, avoiding the need to precompute and store full PF-ODE trajectories. This makes the initialization both more efficient and easier to optimize. The resulting pipeline, \ours, surpasses the SOTA 4-step chunk-wise Causal Forcing under the \textit{\textbf{frame-wise 2-step setting}} by 0.1 in VBench Total, 0.3 in VBench Quality, and 0.335 in VisionReward, while reducing first-frame latency by 50\% and Stage 2 training cost by $\sim$$4\times$. We further extend the pipeline to action-conditioned world model generation in the spirit of Genie3. Project Page: https://github.com/thu-ml/Causal-Forcing and https://github.com/shengshu-ai/minWM .

AI.tldr: Causal Forcing++ is a scalable pipeline for frame-wise autoregressive video generation with only 1-2 sampling steps, using causal consistency distillation to achieve better quality, lower latency, and reduced training cost than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing autoregressive diffusion distillation methods work in chunk-wise 4-step regimes but suffer from coarse response granularity and high latency. The authors aim to achieve frame-wise autoregression with only 1-2 sampling steps, identifying initialization of few-step AR students as the key bottleneck.
AI.method: They propose Causal Forcing++ with causal consistency distillation (causal CD), which learns the same AR-conditional flow map as causal ODE distillation but obtains supervision from a single online teacher ODE step between adjacent timesteps, avoiding precomputation of full PF-ODE trajectories for more efficient and optimizable initialization.
AI.result: Under frame-wise 2-step setting, surpasses SOTA 4-step chunk-wise Causal Forcing by 0.1 in VBench Total, 0.3 in VBench Quality, and 0.335 in VisionReward, while reducing first-frame latency by 50% and Stage 2 training cost by ~4×.
AI.conclusion: Causal Forcing++ enables efficient frame-wise autoregressive video generation with fewer steps, lower latency, and reduced training cost, and extends to action-conditioned world model generation in the spirit of Genie3.

title: Does Synthetic Layered Design Data Benefit Layered Design Decomposition?
comment: 22 pages, 10 figures. Code is available at https://github.com/YangHaolin0526/SynLayers
summary: Recent advances in image generation have made it easy to produce high-quality images. However, these outputs are inherently flattened, entangling foreground elements, background, and text within a fixed canvas. As a result, flexible post-generation editing remains challenging, revealing a clear last-mile gap toward practical usability. Existing approaches either rely on scarce proprietary layered assets or construct partially synthetic data from limited structural priors. However, both strategies face fundamental challenges in scalability. In this work, we investigate whether pure synthetic layered data can improve graphic design decomposition. We make the assumption that, in graphic design, effective decomposition does not require modeling inter-layer dependencies as precisely as in natural-image composition, since design elements are often intentionally arranged as modular and semantically separable components. Concretely, we conduct a data-centric study based on CLD baseline, which is a state-of-the-art layer decomposition framework. Based on the baseline, we construct our own synthetic dataset, SynLayers, generate textual supervision using vision language models, and automate inference inputs with VLM-predicted bounding boxes. Our study reveals three key findings: (1) even training with purely synthetic data can outperform non-scalable alternatives such as the widely used PrismLayersPro dataset, demonstrating its viability as a scalable and effective substitute; (2) performance consistently improves with increased training data scale, while gains begin to saturate at around 50K samples; and (3) synthetic data enables balanced control over layer-count distributions, avoiding the layer-count imbalance commonly observed in real-world datasets. We hope this data-centric study encourages broader adoption of synthetic data as a practical foundation for layered design editing systems.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether pure synthetic layered data can improve graphic design decomposition. Using the CLD baseline, the authors construct SynLayers dataset, finding that synthetic data outperforms non-scalable alternatives like PrismLayersPro, performance saturates around 50K samples, and synthetic data enables balanced control over layer-count distributions.
AI.motivation: Recent advances in image generation produce high-quality images but entangle foreground, background, and text, making post-generation editing challenging. Existing approaches rely on scarce proprietary layered assets or partially synthetic data from limited priors, both facing scalability issues.
AI.method: The authors conduct a data-centric study based on the CLD baseline, constructing a synthetic dataset called SynLayers. They generate textual supervision using vision language models (VLMs) and automate inference inputs with VLM-predicted bounding boxes.
AI.result: Three key findings: (1) purely synthetic data training outperforms non-scalable alternatives like PrismLayersPro; (2) performance improves with more data but saturates around 50K samples; (3) synthetic data enables balanced control over layer-count distributions, mitigating real-world imbalances.
AI.conclusion: The authors hope this data-centric study encourages broader adoption of synthetic data as a practical foundation for layered design editing systems.

title: Evidential Reasoning Advances Interpretable Real-World Disease Screening
summary: Disease screening is critical for early detection and timely intervention in clinical practice. However, most current screening models for medical images suffer from limited interpretability and suboptimal performance. They often lack effective mechanisms to reference historical cases or provide transparent reasoning pathways. To address these challenges, we introduce EviScreen, an evidential reasoning framework for disease screening that leverages region-level evidence from historical cases. The proposed EviScreen offers retrospection interpretability through regional evidence retrieved from dual knowledge banks. Using this evidential mechanism, the subsequent evidence-aware reasoning module makes predictions using both the current case and evidence from historical cases, thereby enhancing disease screening performance. Furthermore, rather than relying on post-hoc saliency maps, EviScreen enhances localization interpretability by leveraging abnormality maps derived from contrastive retrieval. Our method achieves superior performance on our carefully established benchmarks for real-world disease screening, yielding notably higher specificity at clinical-level recall. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/DopamineLcy/EviScreen.

AI.tldr: EviScreen is an evidential reasoning framework for disease screening that uses region-level evidence from historical cases and dual knowledge banks to achieve both retrospection and localization interpretability, outperforming existing methods on real-world benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current disease screening models for medical images suffer from limited interpretability and suboptimal performance, lacking mechanisms to reference historical cases or provide transparent reasoning pathways.
AI.method: EviScreen uses dual knowledge banks for regional evidence retrieval from historical cases, followed by an evidence-aware reasoning module that combines current case features with retrieved evidence. It also leverages contrastive retrieval-based abnormality maps for localization interpretability instead of post-hoc saliency maps.
AI.result: Superior performance on real-world disease screening benchmarks, achieving notably higher specificity at clinical-level recall compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: EviScreen effectively addresses interpretability and performance limitations in disease screening by integrating evidential reasoning with historical case retrieval, and the code is publicly available.

title: SANA-WM: Efficient Minute-Scale World Modeling with Hybrid Linear Diffusion Transformer
summary: We introduce SANA-WM, an efficient 2.6B-parameter open-source world model natively trained for one-minute generation, synthesizing high-fidelity, 720p, minute-scale videos with precise camera control. SANA-WM achieves visual quality comparable to large-scale industrial baselines such as LingBot-World and HY-WorldPlay, while significantly improving efficiency. Four core designs drive our architecture: (1) Hybrid Linear Attention combines frame-wise Gated DeltaNet (GDN) with softmax attention for memory-efficient long-context modeling. (2) Dual-Branch Camera Control ensures precise 6-DoF trajectory adherence. (3) Two-Stage Generation Pipeline applies a long-video refiner to stage-1 outputs, improving quality and consistency across sequences.
